feed.title: SAP Community - Artificial Intelligence
feed.subtitle: Artificial Intelligence blog posts in SAP Community

entry.title: GRC Game: AI vs Human. Result: 6:0. Six months of production SAP GRC Firefighter data study.
entry.content:
Final whistle on six months of SAP Firefighter activity in a real production landscape. Team Human Review: 0. Team AI Agent: 6. Team AI did not win because it was faster than the human side. It won because it played a different game on the same field. This article series walks through what those six “goals” were, how Team AI scored them, and why the score is the more process and approach fault, not necessary so called human errors. The six “goals” scored by AI Agents during the game described across this series:

  • Goal #1. The FF executor vs auditor relationship (peer review audit)
  • Goal #2. The source-of-FF activity (technical accounts under emergency labels)
  • Goal #3. GDPR exposure during FF session (technical field)
  • Goal #4. Missing data in approved FF session (approval judgement)
  • Goal #5. FF emergency account used for mass changes
  • Goal #6. The quality of FF session review (missing critical transaction usage)

This article walks through the six "goals" in order, then the post-match analysis: how Team AI was built, the six-layer architecture behind it, the validation discipline, and the one-week actions Team Human can take to compete on the same field starting Monday.

By Filip Nowak, GRC Advisory. The six signals described in this blog were produced by a GRC AI agent in the GRC LAB on an anonymized extract of a real production SAP S/4HANA Firefighter deployment. Methodology, heuristics, and editorial responsibility rest with the GRC Advisory consulting team.

What this blog is, and is not

This is a data-pattern study, split into three parts for readability. It analyzes an anonymized, production-scale SAP Firefighter activity dataset and describes six classes of control signal visible when the data is examined cross-FF sessions instead on traditional (oriented on one FF activity review). It is not a public audit report. It is not a description of weaknesses at a specific organization. It is not a claim that AI replaces human auditors.

What it does claim: that classical session-by-session Firefighter review is not designed to surface cross-session, cross-user, cross-source patterns; that those patterns are quantifiable in production data of normal scale; and that a GRC AI agent equipped with the right architecture can extract those patterns reliably enough that human auditors can validate and act on them. Every signal was checked, on the underlying data, by an accountable GRC security consultant before it appeared in this series.

The agent detects patterns. The human decides whether the pattern is audit-relevant.

The match-up

Two ways to read a Firefighter session. Session-based review asks: was this individual session legitimate? This is the question every classical Firefighter audit review is structured to answer. The FF reviewer opens the session, reads the reservation reason, scans the transaction log, checks for obvious anomalies, signs off. That is the Team Human playbook.

AI pattern-based analysis started as typical Human review but also additionally asks more and go deeper with different type of question: does the arrangement of sessions, users, sources, transactions, audit statuses and historical baselines creates a risk pattern? This requires cross-referencing data domains the session-based review does not surface together: relationship graphs between requesters and approvers, source-host distributions across accounts, regex-style content of accounting document header text, system-parameter configuration governing change-document logging, concentration ratios for activity per user, dwell-time distributions per transaction risk class. That is the Team AI playbook.

Same data. Same field of play. Two different playbooks. The signals described in this series are visible only when you ask more and different type of questions. The data to answer it exists in tables every SAP and GRC landscape already maintains. The AI review insight does not exist in classical session review. At least so far and untill now. That is the asymmetry that turned the match into AI victory with score 6 to 0.

The match conditions

Six months of continuous observation on a production SAP S/4HANA landscape with multiple company codes. Almost 600 Firefighter reservations across 11 emergency accounts, 39 unique end users. Approximately 2.55 million Security Audit Log entries. Around 352,000 master-data changes. Over 201,000 accounting documents. Approximately 14,000 SAP audit log entries per day.

To be explicit on data origin: the dataset is real production data extracted from a real SAP S/4HANA Firefighter deployment, not a synthetic or test environment. References to “our LAB” point to the analytical environment in which the GRC AI agent processed the anonymized extract, not to the origin of the data. Specific identifiers, account names, country structure, and exact date ranges have been anonymized; orders of magnitude and structural patterns are unchanged.

The volume context matters for the ~600 FF sessions in this dataset. At the typical 5-10% sampling rate, the most qualified and engaged FF log reviewers examine maybe 30 to 60 sessions in detail - spending an enormous amount of time and effort on the activity, trying to find patterns of wrongdoing inside each FF session. The other 530-plus get an 'accept' click. Sampling at 5-10% is not a process choice - it is a resource constraint. At any larger sample size, the audit population would need to expand by an order of magnitude, and the controls that justified keeping it narrow (independence, accountability, audit-trail integrity) would not hold. No reviewer working session-by-session could ever have spotted the first two patterns described below: they only emerge when you compute across the whole dataset. The math and structure of the review do not allow it. The review was not designed to ask the question. This was the field on which Team Human played.

Goal #1. The FF executor vs auditor relationship (peer review audit)

The question this signal answers: when the same population of users both reserves and approves Firefighter sessions, what fraction of accepted sessions form closed loops where heavy users approve each other?

The dataset showed 115 sessions in six months, roughly one in four accepted sessions, were approved by an auditor who themselves was among the heaviest Firefighter operators. Both the requester and the approver belonged to the same operational team in most of these cases. Each individual approval, in isolation, looks defensible. The pattern emerges only by computing the user-auditor graph across all accepted sessions and identifying the closed loops.

Why this matters: a reviewer auditing a teammate's session operates under different incentives than one auditing a stranger. Trust is faster than challenge. Pushing back on a colleague's session creates two costs the reviewer cannot easily absorb. First, friction inside the team that has to keep working together. Second, the reasonable expectation that the same ‘detailed’ inspection will come back the next time their own session is in the queue. Challenge goes uphill. Acceptance goes downhill. The path of least resistance is to sign and move on.

Each individual approval still looks defensible. Process was followed. The requester is authorized to reserve. The auditor is authorized to clear. The trail is clean. On the surface, one person works and another verifies, which reads like control. The systemic risk lives only in the aggregate, where the people most able to scrutinize each other's work also have the least incentive to do so. That picture is not visible in any single session record.

How AI scored this goal: it stopped looking at individual session records and started looking at the network behind them. The agent built a relational model of the entire audit cycle. Every requester as a node. Every approver as a node. Every accepted session as a directed edge from requester to approver. With that graph in working memory, finding the closed loops where heavy users sign off on each other's sessions is a single query, not a 481-record scan. The AI agent also cross-referenced the graph with two contextual layers that the manual workflow keeps in separate tools: the authorization matrix that says who can approve, and the per-user activity baseline that identifies the heavy-user population. The pattern emerged in seconds because the agent was looking at the network structure, not at individual records. That is the move that scored the goal as FF quality and independent review of activity during the FF session is a fundament of this control. No quality review = no control for this risk is in place.

Why Team Human's session-based playbook could not match this: the log entries exist inside the review, but no view inside the workflow lets the reviewer aggregate them by source host across accounts. Aggregating that classification per account and comparing the ratio against a baseline for emergency-versus-technical accounts requires the role catalogue and a heuristic about what proportion suggests technical use. Neither is part of the per-session checklist. The reviewer has the data. They do not have the lens. It also requires the reviewer to think beyond the immediate checklist and ask a different kind of question: not only “was this individual session justified?”, but “what pattern emerges when these sessions are compared across accounts, hosts, and account types?” That is a deeper analytical step than the standard workflow encourages. To be fair, this kind of task is especially difficult for a human reviewer because it involves reading through long review logs for hours, often containing repetitive, low-signal entries. The work is time-consuming, monotonous, and mentally draining. After a certain point, attention naturally drops, and subtle patterns become easy to miss. A reviewer may correctly process each individual entry and still fail to notice the broader signal hidden across many similar sessions. In other words, the limitation is not that the reviewer lacks access to the evidence. The limitation is that the evidence is buried inside a FF workflow optimized for per-session validation, not cross-account pattern detection. The data is present, but the review process does not make the relevant pattern visible.

Goal #2. The source-of-FF activity (technical accounts under emergency labels)

The question this signal answers: what proportion of activity on a given Firefighter account originates on a server hostname versus a user workstation, and which accounts behave as de facto service accounts despite their emergency-access classification?

The dataset showed that 53.7% of all Security Audit Log entries from Firefighter sessions, approximately 1.37 million lines, originated on server hostnames. That is automation running under accounts labeled as emergency access, not human emergency work. Three of the eleven Firefighter accounts showed a source-host distribution dominated by server traffic, indicating they function as de facto technical accounts. The structural recommendation: replace those three accounts with narrowly scoped technical accounts following the Z_BC_* pattern.

On a per-session level, no individual session reveals this pattern. Each session shows a reservation, a list of transactions, and an audit verdict. The fact that the transactions inside the session originated on a server hostname rather than the requester's workstation is buried in a column the audit workflow never groups by. A reviewer who opens the session sees activity that looks legitimate: transactions matching the role scope, a reasonable time window, a clean log. The structural anomaly does not appear at the session level. It is visible only when you classify the log entries by source host and aggregate the result per account. That classification is not in the per-session view.

How AI scored this goal: it stopped reading the audit log as a list of session events and started reading it as a dimensional dataset. The agent computed the source-host distribution across all 2.55 million log entries, broken down by Firefighter account. Within seconds it had a table of eleven accounts, each with a ratio of server-host to workstation activity. Then it cross-referenced that table with two things the manual workflow keeps in separate places: the role catalog (which account is what type) and the SAP industry heuristic (more than half server-host activity equals a service-account workload, not an emergency-access workload). Three Firefighter accounts stood out as behaving like technical accounts in disguise. The agent then produced the structural recommendation: reclassify those three accounts to the Z_BC_* pattern with narrow scope, not "tell the reviewer to look closer next time". That is the move that scored the goal.

Why Team Human's session-based playbook could not match this: the log entries exist inside the review, but no view inside the workflow lets the reviewer aggregate them by source host across accounts. Aggregating that classification per account and comparing the ratio against a baseline for emergency-versus-technical accounts requires the role catalogue and a heuristic about what proportion suggests technical use. Neither is part of the per-session checklist. The reviewer has the data. They do not have the lens.

Goal #3. GDPR exposure during FF session (technical field)

The question this signal answers: do fields designated for system references contain personally identifiable information that should live elsewhere?

AI Agent review of the dataset showed 19 posted accounting documents in which the BKPF header text field contained customer email addresses on consumer mail providers. Header text on a financial document is designed for system references: invoice IDs, internal correlation keys, document chain pointers. Customer contact data appearing there is a GDPR concern. Personal data is now living in a system field that has no retention policy of its own, is not registered in the organization’s Records of Processing Activities as a PII repository, and is regularly exported in standard reporting flows including audit submissions and partner data shares.

What is actually broken: BKPF-BKTXT is officially classified as “technical reference field” in the data inventory. That classification means DSAR queries (GDPR Art. 15) skip it, erasure requests (Art. 17) skip it, retention policies skip it, and pseudonymisation rules in data exports skip it. Nineteen customer email addresses are now embedded in a 5-plus year accounting record that nobody scans for personal data, in a field nobody looks at when honoring a customer’s data rights. The organization cannot demonstrate compliance with GDPR Art. 5, 15, 17, or 30 for this data class, and does not know that it cannot.

Why per-session FF review did not catch this: when an auditor opens an FF session and reviews the posted documents, they see header text as a string. Even if they mentally register that an entry looks like a customer contact, the audit checklist for FF session review has no item that says “if you see a PII-shaped string in BKPF-BKTXT, raise a control finding”. The reviewer has no follow-up action defined. The field is classified as technical reference, so anything appearing there is treated as a technical reference by default. Human failed to identify this as a problem not because of inattention, but because the review protocol does not assign GDPR compliance scanning to the FF audit step, and because awareness that this information should not be disclosed in posted documents is GDPR consultant knowledge, not part of the standard FF reviewer training.

How AI scored this goal: it ran a content-pattern scan that the manual FF review does not include. The agent took the full set of BKPF-BKTXT values from FF-posted documents, tens of thousands of strings, and applied a regex pattern library covering email addresses, phone numbers, identification numbers, and other personal-data formats. Within seconds it had 19 documents flagged. Then it applied a GRC consultant heuristic that the standard checklist does not encode: when personal data appears in fields not registered as PII repositories, surface it as a control gap and link it to the FF session that posted it. The agent then connected the finding to GDPR Art. 5 (data minimisation), Art. 15 (right of access), Art. 17 (right to erasure), and Art. 30 (records of processing), producing not just nineteen documents but a defensible control statement an internal auditor can take to the DPO. That is the move that scored the goal. PII handling under FF is a control area GDPR-compliant operations must demonstrably manage. No targeted scan of free-text fields means no evidence that this control is operating.

Why Team Human could not score against this: no human auditor reviewing FF sessions one at a time runs a regex match for PII patterns across the BKPF header text field, and the per-session review surfaces individual strings but does not aggregate them for scanning. The heuristic that says “when personal data appears in fields designated for system references, surface it as a control gap” is part of senior GRC consultant practice mapped to specific GDPR articles, not part of the standard FF audit checklist. The reviewer has the strings in front of them. They do not have the scanner, the PII pattern library, or the GDPR-article mapping that would make the strings actionable as a compliance finding.

Goal #4. Missing data in approved FF session (approval judgement)

The question this signal answers: is the FF audit log capturing enough detail to support post-hoc verification of mass-data changes, and if not, where does the gap actually live?

The dataset showed that for approximately 297,000 material master changes performed by the highest-volume FF user, the details field in the CDPOS change-document entries was empty. Not partial. Not garbled. Empty. The standard interpretation of this gap is that mass-data operations do not produce field-level change documents, and that detail-level verification is therefore impossible after the fact.

What is actually broken: ITGC frameworks (SOX, ISO 27001 A.12.4) require that changes to data driving financial outcomes be traceable at field level. 297,000 changes logged as “unable to verify” is not a partial gap; it is a complete failure of field-level change control over the largest single block of master-data activity in the cycle. An auditor cannot conclude operating effectiveness on a control where 297,000 evaluation entries return “unable to verify”. Yet the per-session review treats each entry as a known limitation, the working note stays “unable to verify”, and the cumulative finding is never raised at platform level.

The deeper interpretation, surfaced on a feedback-loop pass of the analysis, is that this is not a logging behavior problem at user level. It is a system parameter configuration problem at platform level. The SAP parameter governing field-level change-document capture for mass MM02 and LSMW updates is most likely not enabled in the landscape. The fix is not “tell auditors to review more carefully”. The fix is in SAP configuration. Nobody noticed or requested it, because asking for it would make an already-impossible review task harder. Under conditions where 300,000 entries are sitting in the queue, the review quality drops by volume alone. Even working 24/7 the verification cannot be completed. It is hard to expect anyone to ask for even more data to be captured there. It is easier to “accept” the review with the gap and move the next session forward.

How AI scored this goal: it reframed the problem with help from a feedback-loop pass. On the first pass through the dataset, the agent flagged the empty details field as a verification gap, the same interpretation a human reviewer would reach. On a later pass, with the heuristic layer enhanced by a GRC consultant correction, the agent asked a different question: if the gap is structural (it repeats across 297,000 changes from one user), what platform-level setting controls field-level change-document capture for mass MM02 and LSMW operations? It cross-referenced SAP Basis configuration parameters, which the manual FF review does not include in its checklist, and identified that the relevant parameter is most likely not enabled. The agent then connected the finding to SOX ITGC and ISO 27001 A.12.4 logging requirements, producing not just a session-level note but a platform-level control finding internal audit can route to the Basis team for remediation. That is the move that scored the goal. FF audit cannot demonstrate the operating effectiveness of mass-data change controls without field-level capture being enabled. No config means no audit evidence means no defensible control.

Why Team Human could not score against this: auditors had logged the empty-details field in their working notes as “unable to verify” across many sessions. Correlating that observation with the SAP configuration parameter that controls field-level audit logging requires both SAP Basis expertise and an audit-control framing. Both sit in the consultant heuristics layer, not in the per-session reviewer’s checklist. The reviewer has the symptom in front of them. They do not have the Basis knowledge plus the framing to convert “I cannot verify” into “the configuration choice is the control gap, and here is the parameter”.

The fix is not “tell auditors to review more carefully”. The fix is in system configuration.

Goal #5. FF emergency account used for mass changes

The question this signal answers: how is Firefighter activity distributed across the user population over six months, and does any single profile’s work pattern match a service-account signature rather than a human power-user signature?

The dataset showed one user profile accounted for 36% of all Firefighter Security Audit Log entries, 85% of all FF master-data changes (299,017 records), and 99.8% of all FF-posted accounting documents (201,413 of 201,840). Three independent measures of FF activity, all pointing to the same profile. The math is not subtle.

What is actually broken: FF is by definition emergency access. Exceptional. Time-limited. Reservation-based. If one profile executes 99.8% of all FF accounting activity in a six-month window, the access is not emergency for that profile. It is permanent elevated access in disguise. The compensating control built on the assumption “FF equals exception” stops being a control the moment FF equals routine. SoD limitations in the user’s normal role no longer apply to the actual workload, because the actual workload runs through the FF path. SOX and ITGC audit treats “emergency access used for routine operations” as an automatic control deficiency. ISO 27001 A.9.2.5 (Review of user access rights) requires privileged accounts be reviewed against actual need. A profile generating 99.8% of accounting documents under FF clearly needs standing access, not emergency access, but the review process has no mechanism to convert that need into a standing-role provisioning decision.

The work pattern itself matches a batch-interface signature, not a human power-user signature. High volume across hundreds of thousands of transactions. Repetitive transaction codes within a narrow scope. No exploratory clicks. No transaction-failure entries. Predictable execution windows. Either a human is executing 200,000-plus transactions manually, which is physically impossible, or some form of automation is running under this human credential through the FF access. Both interpretations are control gaps. The organization either does not know it is running batch automation through emergency access, or it knows and has accepted that emergency access is the daily operational path for one of its most active users.

Why this concentration was never raised: nobody designed it. It accumulated over years, month by month. Year one this user did perhaps 30% of master-data work because they were the most experienced. Year two 50% because newer team members deferred to them. Year three 85% because by then nobody else remembered how to do it. By the time someone looks at the six-month aggregate, the concentration is a single-point dependency dressed up as expertise. The team labels this person an irreplaceable senior contributor. The audit population labels them a heavy user, reviewed correctly. The FF review process has no metric called “your share of cycle volume”, and no signal that says this concentration is mathematically inconsistent with a human power-user profile. The drift is invisible at any single review cycle, and the social framing of “she or he is our master-data expert” protects it from being questioned.

How AI scored this goal: it computed concentration ratios across multiple dimensions of FF activity (log entries per user, master-data changes per user, accounting documents per user), and compared the resulting distribution against a library of operational signatures. Within seconds it had a ranking placing this single profile at 36%, 85%, and 99.8% across the three measures. Then it applied a GRC consultant heuristic the manual FF review does not encode: when a single profile’s distribution matches a batch-interface signature (high volume, narrow transaction range, repeatable cadence, near-zero failure rate, predictable windows), reclassify it as a service-account candidate, not a heavy user. The agent produced the structural recommendation: build a standing technical role with Z_BTC_* narrow scope and migrate this workload out of FF entirely. It connected the finding to SOX ITGC (emergency access used for routine operations is a deficiency), ISO 27001 A.9.2.5 (privileged access review), and ISO 22301 (business continuity, since single-person dependency for 99.8% of accounting throughput is a documented continuity risk). That is the move that scored the goal. Three control frameworks point to the same fix: take this workload off FF, put it on a standing technical role, restore FF to its exception path.

Why Team Human could not score against this: each session opened by this user, reviewed in isolation, looks like legitimate operational work. Transactions match the role scope. Reservation reasons reference real tickets. The audit verdict is “accept”. Nothing in any individual session reveals that the same person is responsible for 99.8% of all FF accounting documents in the dataset. Concentration ratios are a property of the entire dataset, not of any one session. The session log does not carry “your contribution to the cycle aggregate is 99.8%”. Even when reviewers know intuitively that this person is “always on FF”, quantifying that intuition requires computing per-user-share metrics across multiple activity dimensions and comparing the result against a library of batch-interface signatures. Neither is part of per-session review. The reviewer sees the volume. They do not see the concentration ratio, and they do not have the pattern library to distinguish heavy-user volume from automation-shaped volume.

The fix is not “review the heavy user more carefully”. The fix is to recognize batch-interface workload for what it is and remove it from FF entirely.

Goal #6. The quality of FF session review (missing critical transaction usage)

The question this signal answers: do FF sessions containing transactions of differing risk class receive differentiated handling in the review queue, or does a high-risk transaction wait the same number of days for review as a low-risk one?

The dataset showed nine sessions executed transaction F110 (Payment Run, which generates real bank payment orders that move money out of the company the same day). Six of those nine were still in PENDING audit status weeks or months after the session closed. In this dataset, the F110 pattern concentrated around one finance profile. Total F110 executions across the nine sessions: 23. Number of those 23 independently reviewed by the time of analysis: zero. Across the broader dataset, the dwell time of high-risk transactions in PENDING exceeded the dwell time of low-risk ones, the inverse of what an audit policy would intend.

What is actually broken: F110 is the SAP transaction that generates real bank payment orders. Each execution authorizes an outbound payment that leaves the company’s account the same business day. ITGC frameworks (SOX, COBIT) classify payment authorization as a primary control. ISO 27001 A.9.4.4 explicitly addresses control over privileged utility programs, and F110 is a textbook example. Twenty-three F110 executions with zero independent review means the four-eyes principle around outbound payments is not operating. The control on paper says “F110 sessions require timely independent review”. The control in practice says “F110 sessions sit in PENDING longer than routine sessions because nobody volunteers to be the second pair of eyes on a real bank payment”.

Why per-session FF review let high-risk sessions sit longer: it is a risk-aversion paradox. The mental cost of clearing an F110 session is asymmetric. A wrongly cleared F110 review attaches the auditor’s name to a real bank payment that should not have happened. A deferred F110 review costs nothing visible. The rational individual response, given that asymmetry, is to defer until “I have time to do it properly”. But every reviewer makes the same calculation, on the same day, about the same kind of session. The cohort behavior produces an inverted SLA without anyone in the cohort intending it. Add to that the operational truth that F110 sessions take longer to review because the analysis is heavier (more transactions, more amounts, more invoice references to check), so they linger. And the queue does not surface the cohort effect to any individual reviewer.

How AI scored this goal: it correlated transaction risk class with review-status duration across the entire backlog. The agent built a distribution of “days in PENDING” segmented by the highest-risk transaction in the session (F110, SE38, SU01, SM30, all known high-risk codes), and compared it against the distribution for routine sessions. The result was the inversion: high-risk sessions averaged longer dwell time than low-risk ones. Then the agent applied a GRC consultant heuristic the manual checklist does not encode: high-risk transactions must have shorter SLA in PENDING, not longer. The agent produced the structural recommendation: add a transaction-risk-class field to the FF review queue ordering, auto-escalate sessions containing high-risk codes to a four-eyes review track with a defined SLA, route them out of the default PENDING queue, and report SLA compliance separately. It connected the finding to SOX ITGC (payment authorization control), ISO 27001 A.9.4.4 (privileged utility programs), and the four-eyes principle. That is the move that scored the goal. Bank payments executed under FF without timely independent review is exactly the control failure auditors look for in external assessment. No risk-class routing means F110 sits in the same queue as F-04, and the highest-risk activity gets the least time.

Why Team Human could not score against this: each session is reviewed when an auditor opens it. The review queue does not communicate to the auditor whether this session is more or less time-critical than others in the queue, nor what the cohort-level SLA pattern looks like. The dwell-time distribution is not visible inside any session, it is a property of the queue, and the queue does not surface it. Even when an individual reviewer knows that F110 sessions tend to wait too long, they have no quantitative signal to escalate, and no mechanism to route the F110 queue differently from the routine queue.

The fix is not “tell auditors to clear F110 sessions faster”. The fix is to route them out of the default queue and into a four-eyes track with a defined SLA.

Six goals scored. Before turning to the post-match analysis of how Team AI was actually built, it is worth being explicit about what the agent deliberately did not surface.

The goals Team AI did not score

Worth surfacing what the agent did not surface. It did not flag any individual session as fraudulent. The dataset contained no evidence of bad actors. It did not invent compliance findings that were not there. It did not propose remediations the policy layer does not support. It did not, despite running on a model that is fully capable of hallucination, hallucinate transactions, document numbers, or user activity. The numbers in this series appear because the agent counted directly from CDPOS, BKPF and similar tables, not because it estimated.

This is the practical answer to the standard objection to AI in audit contexts. Hallucination happens when a model is asked to reason about something it has no grounded data for.

The agent in this experiment had grounded data for everything it said. The heuristics layer told it what to look for. The tool layer let it pull verified counts from the underlying platform. The feedback layer from previous runs corrected its biases. The output the agent could not ground in the data, it did not surface.

This is not the same as a hallucination-free guarantee. It is a verifiability guarantee: every count the agent did surface is reproducible by re-running the same query against the same tables.

The model is the smallest part of an AI agent in audit. The architecture around it is what determines whether the output is defensible.

How Team AI trains: methodology and validation

The heuristics layer the agent reasons over was authored by senior GRC consultants with 15+ years of SAP GRC AC audit experience, peer-reviewed against the firm’s audit training material, and version-controlled. Each heuristic must reference a documented audit-control rationale before it is committed to the agent’s reasoning library. Heuristics are not added by prompt iteration; they are added by the same process by which a consulting firm would write a new internal control.

Every signal the agent produced was reviewed by a human GRC AC consultant before it appeared in this series. Two candidate signals from earlier analysis passes were rejected in review: one was a false positive based on incomplete role-catalogue data (the agent had been working with a stale snapshot from before a routine SoD redesign), and one was a real anomaly that did not have audit-relevant implications and would have produced noise rather than signal. The six signals published all passed that human review.

False-positive rate across all analysis passes ran at approximately 8%. Roughly one in twelve agent-flagged items was rejected by the human reviewer as either based on incomplete context or as a benign outlier. The agent’s confidence score correlates with reviewer acceptance, but is not a substitute for review. Every signal in this series was checked, on the underlying data, by an accountable GRC consultant. No signal is presented as definitive without that human sign-off.

The division of labour is the principle. The agent detects patterns at a scale and across data domains that human review cannot reach: pattern detection across 600 sessions, ratio computation across 2.55 million log entries, cross-referencing across multiple data domains. The human decides whether the pattern is audit-relevant, whether the framing is correct, and whether it is publishable as a control claim. Neither replaces the other.

How Team AI lines up: the six-layer architecture

A tool layer: each function of the GRC platform exposed as a well-typed API the agent can call without hallucinating its arguments. Every action bounded, type-checked, and reversible.

A policy layer: Firefighter rules, SoD ruleset, transaction risk classes, audit comment templates, four-eyes routing rules. All in a structured format the agent queries, rather than free-text the model has to interpret.

A historical context layer: every past audit verdict with its comment, every reservation reason with its outcome, indexed and queryable.

A heuristics layer: the GRC consultant know-how about real anomalies versus benign outliers. The most difficult layer to build, because the authors are senior consultants, not LLM engineers.

A feedback layer: corrections from previous runs that adjust the agent’s bias on the next pass.

And on top of all of it, a deterministic verification step that re-runs every count the agent produces, so the output is reproducible end-to-end on demand.

This is what AI in a GRC platform means as a product category. Not a ChatGPT box added to the UI. The same audit functions the platform already has, exposed as agent tools, sitting under layers of business-audit logic and historical context that structurally constrain hallucination and make every agent claim verifiable.

A different vendor’s GRC platform (SAP GRC, others in this class) could build the same architecture. In our implementation, these capabilities are delivered through the smartAccess module of smartGRC. The architecture is platform-neutral.

The discipline of keeping the agent grounded in verifiable data, rather than marketing the hallucination problem away, is what will define the next five years of SAP GRC.

How Team Human gets back into the game

Measure your own pitch

The signals are reproducible. They live in data your organization already has, in tables your GRC platform already queries. The following questions can be answered against your own Firefighter dataset with the same tool layer the agent in this experiment used: change-document tables, accounting document headers, audit log entries, reservation history, role catalogues. For the relationship signal: of all Firefighter sessions accepted in your last cycle, what fraction were approved by a member of your top decile of heavy users?

For the relationship signal (Goal #1): build a directed graph of FF reservations where each requester is a node, each approver is a node, and every accepted session is an edge from requester to approver. What fraction of accepted sessions form closed loops where heavy FF users sign off on each other’s sessions?

For the source-of-activity signal: per Firefighter account, what is the ratio of audit log entries originating on server hostnames versus user workstations? Which accounts cross the threshold that suggests service-account workload?

For the data-placement signal: a regex search across BKPF header text for the last six months. How many documents contain email addresses or other personal data formats?

For the logging-completeness signal: is the SAP system parameter controlling field-level change-document capture for mass MM02 and LSMW operations enabled in your landscape?

For the concentration signal: per Firefighter user profile, what is their share of total accounting documents and master-data changes?

For the review-priority signal (Goal #6): in your current PENDING queue, what is the dwell-time distribution of sessions containing F110, SE38, SU01, or SM30 versus the dwell time of routine sessions?

Each of these queries is a one-week task for a competent SAP/GRC analyst. The patterns they surface, if any, will not be the same numbers as in this series. Different landscapes produce different distributions. The questions are the same.

Three one-week actions

The signals translate into concrete one-week actions for different roles in the GRC operation, independent of any structural multi-year change program. A CISO should commission a source-of-activity audit on the organization’s own Firefighter accounts. A one-day query against the Security Audit Log will produce the same kind of percentage as Goal 2, or a different one. If the number is high, some emergency accounts may be operating as technical accounts in disguise.

A GRC Manager should add a transaction-risk-class field to the audit workflow’s PENDING queue ordering, mapping Goal 6 to a review-queue configuration change rather than a project. The Firefighter policy you wrote already classifies F110 as high-risk; the workflow needs to read that classification.

An internal auditor should request from the SAP Basis team confirmation of whether the field-level change-document parameter (Goal 4) is enabled in the landscape, and treat the answer as a control-evaluation finding either way.

Each of these is a one-week action, not a multi-year program.

The bigger match

The question is not whether to put AI in GRC. The question is whether GRC platforms will expose their audit functions as agent tools the way they exposed them as human UIs ten years ago, and whether the heuristics GRC consultants carry in their heads will be encoded into a context layer an agent can reason over consistently. The first vendors to do that, with the discipline to keep the agent grounded in verifiable data rather than marketing the hallucination problem away, will define what the next five years of SAP GRC look like.

Whether your audit cycle finds these patterns before they appear in an external auditor’s report depends entirely on which tools you give the people responsible for finding them. The signals in this series were the agent’s first deliverable. The structural fixes they point at are now everyone’s problem to solve.

About this series. The dataset analyzed in this article comes from a real production SAP S/4HANA Firefighter deployment; the analysis was conducted by GRC Advisory’s smartGRC LAB on an anonymized extract of that data. The article does not name, identify, or attribute the analysis to any specific organization. If you operate SAP Firefighter at scale and want to discuss running a similar analysis on your own data, please comment the research and study.

entry.title: Can AI Tell Your FI Ticket from Your MM Ticket?
entry.content:
If you've spent any time in an SAP support team, you've lived this moment: a ticket lands with a subject like "PO stuck, please help urgently" and no indication of whether it's a Materials Management problem, a Finance problem, or a CPI interface quietly failing in the background. Someone — usually a senior consultant with better things to do — has to read it, guess the module, and reroute it. Multiply that by a few hundred tickets a month and you've got a real cost hiding in plain sight.

That's the question behind sap-ticket-classifier-bert: can a small, purpose-built AI model handle that first triage step instead of a person? Try it right now:

Live demo: https://karthionlupenix-sap-ticket-classifier-app.hf.space/

Model card: https://huggingface.co/KarthionLupenix/sap-ticket-classifier-bert

The problem with "just use ChatGPT for this"

It's tempting to point a large general-purpose LLM at every problem. But ticket routing is a narrow, repetitive, high-volume classification task — exactly the case where a small fine-tuned model beats a giant one on cost, latency, and predictability. You don't need a model that can write poetry to know that "GR/IR mismatch on vendor invoice" is an FI ticket. You need something fast, cheap to run, and consistent.

So the approach here is deliberately old-school: take bert-base-uncased, add a classification head, and fine-tune it specifically on SAP support ticket language.

Figure 1: SAP ticket classification flow using a fine-tuned BERT model

Nine buckets, one blind spot solved

The model sorts tickets into nine categories: FI, MM, SD, HR, ABAP, BASIS, INTEGRATION, OTHERS, and UNKNOWN.

That INTEGRATION category is the one most home-grown ticket systems get wrong. Middleware issues — a CPI iFlow failing, an IDoc stuck in status 51, an OData service timing out — don't cleanly belong to any one functional module, so they get bounced around before landing with the right team. Giving integration its own first-class category means those tickets get to the CPI/PI team immediately instead of on the third handoff.

How well does it actually work?

On held-out test data, the model hits 82.4% overall accuracy and an 82.6% weighted F1 score. Some categories are close to solved — HR, INTEGRATION, and UNKNOWN tickets are classified correctly more than 94% of the time. The harder categories are MM and SD, sitting around 60–70% F1, which honestly matches reality: a lot of tickets genuinely straddle both (a delivery pricing issue touches MM and SD at once), so some confusion there isn't a bug, it's the nature of the taxonomy.

The part that matters more than the accuracy number

Any model trained on a small seed dataset will make mistakes once it meets real-world tickets. So the demo isn't a one-shot prediction tool — it has a built-in correction flow. When the model gets something wrong, you tell it the right answer on the spot. Those corrections get folded back into training with extra weight, so the model is continuously nudged toward the mistakes it's actually making, not hypothetical ones.

That loop — predict, correct, retrain — is the difference between a cute demo and something you'd trust to sit in front of a real ticket queue.

Try it on your own tickets

Paste any ticket text into the live demo and watch it break down confidence across all nine modules. Or call it directly:

from transformers import pipeline

classifier = pipeline(
    "text-classification",
    model="KarthionLupenix/sap-ticket-classifier-bert"
)

classifier("MRP run not generating planned orders for plant 1000")
# → MM

Where this goes next

The current version is trained on 369 examples — enough to prove the concept, not yet enough for blind production trust. The next step is fine-tuning it on real, anonymized ticket history from live SAP CIG/CPI integration work, which should push accuracy up meaningfully on the trickier MM/SD boundary cases.

If your team is sitting on years of SAP ticket history and still routing tickets by hand, this is a small proof that you don't need a massive model to fix that — you need the right labels, a focused model, and a feedback loop that keeps it honest.

Author: If you find this information helpful, please consider clicking the "Like" button on this blog and sharing your thoughts in the comments section below. You can also connect with the author on their LinkedIn profile: [Author's LinkedIn Profile]

entry.title: How to Connect Claude Code to ABAP with Zero-Config MCP
entry.content:
Before We Start - What This Post Deliberately Skips
- What is Claude Code, and what are the available models?
- What is MCP (Model Context Protocol) and how it works conceptually?
- What is vibe coding and why AI-assisted development matters?
- What are the benefits of using AI in SAP development?
- What development platforms are available (Web, Desktop, CLI)?
If you need more details, you can find these topics in sap community portal. Read that, then come back here.

What This Post Covers - and Why It Matters?

This post walks you through connecting Claude Code to a live SAP system using the ABAP Remote Filesystem extension's native MCP server - no external Node.js setup, no manual JSON editing, just a single toggle. 

What this changes for SAP development:

  • No more hand-typed routine code. TYPE declarations, SELECT statements, ALV setup - written directly into the SAP object from a single prompt.
  • No more AI guesswork. Without a live connection, AI tools hallucinate tables and function modules that don't exist. With MCP, every suggestion is grounded in your actual system.
  • No more setup overhead. What used to require a custom Node.js project now takes one checkbox and one command - open to any developer with VS Code.
  • Clean Core by default. Claude works through the same ADT API and released objects that Eclipse uses, so generated code fits your governance model instead of fighting it.

This is the point where AI-assisted ABAP stops being a chat-window experiment and starts being a real part of your IDE.

 
Goal: I will cover the entire topic in 4 parts:
|Part 1| Installing Claude Code on Windows and setting up the VS Code extension |
|Part 2| Connecting VS Code to your live SAP system via the ABAP FS extension |
|Part 3| Enabling the native MCP server with a single toggle - no Node.js setup, no JSON editing |
|Part 4| A live demo: one plain-English prompt creates a real, running ABAP report on SAP |
If you're here, you already know why this matters. Let's get to the actual steps.
 
Pre-requisite: This guide assumes you already have an active Claude subscription (Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise). If not, sign up at claude.ai before proceeding
 
Part 1 - The Setup: Getting Claude Code Running on Windows
Step 1.1 - Install Claude Code CLI
Open PowerShell (the built-in Windows command-line shell) and run:

 

This downloads Anthropic's official installer and executes it in one step. 'irm' fetches the script, 'iex' runs it.
> ⚠️ Common Error - Git Bash Not Found
> Claude Code needs a bash shell to run on Windows. If you see this error, install Git for Windows from [git-scm.com/downloads/win](https://git-scm.com/downloads/win). During setup, select: Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software. Then re-run the install command above.
Step 1.2 — Verify the Installation

 

Close PowerShell and open a fresh window. Run:
You should see a version number like 'claude 1.x.x'. If PowerShell says claude is not recognized, the PATH wasn't set automatically. Fix it manually:

 

1. Start → search Environment Variables in system settings
2. Edit the environment variables for your account
3. Select Path → Edit → New → paste ( in this case path can be taken from powershell commnad prompt)
 for eg: 

 

4. Close and reopen PowerShell, then re-run claude --version.

> Run claude doctor - this command checks your PATH, authentication, Node.js version, and config in one shot. Always the first step when something isn't working.
Step 1.3 - Launch Claude Code and the VS Code Extension
Navigate to your project folder and launch Claude Code.
The first launch opens your browser to authenticate with your Anthropic account (Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise all work). 
For everyday ABAP work, the VS Code extension is where you'll spend most of your time.
Download and Install VS code from code.visualstudio.com for windows if you don't already have it. Once it is installed.Open VS Code, press 'Ctrl+Shift+X', search for Claude Code (published by Anthropic), and install it. Once installed, the Claude icon appears in the Activity Bar and connects automatically to your account.

 

The extension gives you a persistent chat panel inside VS Code - ask Claude about the file you have open, highlight any ABAP block and ask it to explain or refactor, and switch between ABAP and CAP projects in the same session. No terminal switching required.
> Note on Software Center installs: If your organization restricts installations, Claude Code for VS Code is typically available via your company Software Center / Company Portal.
 
Part 2 - Connecting VS Code to Your SAP System via ABAP FS
This is where the magic begins. The ABAP Remote Filesystem (ABAP FS)extension - built by Marcello Urbani - connects VS Code to your SAP system using the same ADT REST API that Eclipse uses. Once connected, your ABAP objects (programs, classes, CDS views, behavior definitions) appear as files in VS Code. Claude Code can then read, write, and review them like any local code file.
Pre-requisites Before You Connect
You'll need these four things from your SAP system before starting:
| Hostname or IP | The address of your SAP server (hostname preferred - IP causes SSL issues explained below) |
| ADT Service active | Go to transaction SICF → '/sap/bc/adt' must be activated |
| Client number| e.g. '100' - visible in the SAP logon screen |
|Username & Password| Your regular SAP development user |
| Port | HTTPS: '44300' (typical) | HTTP: '8000' for instance 00, '8001' for instance 01 |
 
Step 2.1 - Install and Configure ABAP FS
1. In VS Code Extensions ('Ctrl+Shift+X'), search ABAP remote filesystem by Marcello Urbani → Install.
2. Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → type ABAP FS: Connection Manager → open it.
3. Add a new connection and fill in the fields:
   - Name: anything you like (e.g. DEV or SANDBOX)
   - Server URL:'https://<hostname>:44300'
   - ⚠️ Do NOT add '/sap/bc/adt' to the URL - the extension adds this automatically. If you include it, the path doubles and the connection fails.
   - Client, Username, Language: 'EN'
4. Save.
5. Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → ABAP FS: Connect to an SAP system→ select your connection → enter password.
 

 

> ⚠️ SSL Certificate Error (very common on internal systems)
> If you see IP does not match certificate's altnames - this is because your SAP system's SSL certificate was issued with a hostname, not the IP. The fix: Get the hostname (e.g. 'https://sapdev.company.com:44300') and use that instead of the IP. 
> ⚠️ADT Service Not Found
> If you see Resource /sap/bc/adt does not exist - the ADT service isn't activated. Run tcode SICF → navigate to default_host → 'sap/bc/adt' → right-click → Activate Service.
 
Step 2.2 - Navigating ABAP Objects
ABAP FS gives you two ways to find objects. Object Search lets you find anything by name instantly. If you prefer a tree view like Eclipse ADT, enable SAP GUI Integration in your connection settings - this gives you a full package tree (Dictionary, Source Code Library, Transactions) and even embeds SAP GUI for HTML directly inside VS Code, so you can run transactions without leaving the editor.
1. Click OBJECT SEARCH in the left sidebar
2. Type the object or package name (e.g. 'ZCL_MY_CLASS' or 'Z_MY_PACKAGE')
3. Double-click any result to open it as a text file in VS Code
4. To edit: right-click  → edit → save (changes push back to SAP automatically)
Once an object is open in VS Code, open the integrated terminal (Ctrl+) and run claude. Some prompts that immediately pay off:

 

Part 3 -The Game Changer: Enabling the Native MCP Server

 

This is the step that changes everything - and the reason this post exists.
Why ABAP FS Alone Isn't Enough?
With only ABAP FS connected, Claude can see only the one file you currently have open. Ask it to create a new class and it might invent table names, function modules, or BAdI definitions that don't exist on your system. It's helpful, but it's guessing.
          ABAP FS Only                                         ABAP FS + MCP
Claude sees what we search Manually Claude autonomously searches any object on the system
Hallucinates objects it hasn't seen Grounded in live SAP system data
You copy-paste code manually Claude reads, writes, activates autonomously
No transport awareness Creates & assigns transports via ADT

 

The MCP (Model Context Protocol) server gives Claude Code a live connection to your SAP system's ADT API - the same backend Eclipse uses. Claude stops guessing and starts knowing.
Step 3.1 - Enable the MCP Server (One Toggle)
This is where the zero-config part comes in. You don't need to install anything extra, clone a Node.js repo, or edit JSON files manually.
Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → search ABAP FS MCP → select it → enable Autostart.
That's it. The ABAP FS extension spins up a local MCP server on your machine (default port 4847). You'll see a VS Code notification confirming it's running:
> ABAP MCP Server running on port 4847. External AI clients can connect to http://localhost:4847/mcp

Step 3.2 - Register the MCP Server with Claude Code
Now tell Claude Code where to find the ABAP tools. Run this in the VS Code integrated terminal:
This registers the local MCP server with Claude Code. The configuration is saved to your '.claude.json' file and persists across sessions - you only need to do this once.
Step 3.3 - Verify the Connection

 

open the terminal and run this command once the MCP server is added 
You should see:
Claude Code will confirm when the server is registered:
> abap-remote-fs has been added with HTTP transport at http://localhost:4847/mcp"*
The Complete Architecture
Claude Code can now autonomously:
- Search and read any ABAP object on the live SAP system
- write → syntax check → activate → unlock objects
- Create transport requests and assign objects
- Run ATC checks and read short dumps - all from a plain-English prompt
> Golden - In Auto mode, Claude will write, syntax check, and activate without pausing - that's the power of the agentic workflow. If you want to review before anything is activated, switch to Plan mode  shift+tab to cycle modes)  Claude will show you the full plan and wait for your approval. For production-adjacent systems, always run in Plan or Step mode.
Part 4 - Live Demo: One Prompt, One Real ABAP Program on SAP
Disclaimer: This is my first time setting up all this and explore, so i just started with a very simple scenario. Going forward will create RAP objects and other complex artifacts and see the efficiency.
Let me show you what all of this enables. Here's the exact prompt I used:
> Create a new ABAP executable program ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01 in my ZVS_DEMO package. Fetch data from MARA for a user-specified Material Number (select-option) and display Material Number, Type, and Created By in an ALV grid. Write in ABAP 7.5 syntax.
One prompt. No templates. No copy-paste. Here's what Claude did.
Act 1 - Claude Plans Before It Codes
Claude didn't immediately start writing code. It:
1. Confirmed the target package ('ZVS_DEMO') exists on the system
2. Detected that the program creation triggered a **transport request dialog** — and recognised it was the likely source of a cancellation
3. Called the SAP ADT API via Bash to **list existing open transports**
4. Selected the correct transport and retried the creation
There was a little bit delay on my end to confirm the transport. and it gave me this message.
> Package confirmed. The cancellation is likely a transport request dialog. Let me check for existing transports, then retry.
This is the part that impresses ABAP developers most. Claude handled a real SAP workflow quirk .
Act 2 - The Program Appears on SAP
Claude then created the program on the live system. You can watch its autonomous todo list mid-execution:
- Create ABAP program ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01 in ZVS_DEMO package via MCP
- Get workspace URI for the new program
- Write ABAP source code into the program
- Activate the program and check diagnostics
'ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01' appeared in the VS Code Explorer sidebar - a real, registered ABAP program on the SAP system, not a local file.
You can see initially there was no program created in my package 
After few seconds, the program is created 

 

Act 3 - Full ABAP 7.5 Source Code Written
With the object created, Claude fetched the workspace URI and wrote the complete source code directly into the SAP program. What it generated:
No placeholder code. Real, working ABAP in the correct syntax release, referencing actual MARA field types from the live system.
Act 4 - Running the Program
After activation, there are two ways to run it:
Option A - Classic SAP GUI:Log in to SAP GUI →run the program → Execute
Option B - Embedded SAP Web GUI(what I used): VS Code can open the SAP Web GUI directly in a browser tab, embedded inside the IDE. No separate login or window switching required.
Enter selection criteria:
The selection screen appeared with the Matnr  field exactly as specified. I entered a range of 11to 21. The value help (F4) shows the available materials in the system
Material Number, Material Type, and Created By - exactly what was asked for. Live data from MARA. Report running.
 
Summary:
|Claude understands SAP context| It identified the transport dialog, queried existing transports via ADT, and handled a real SAP workflow quirk autonomously 
| It created a real SAP object| 'ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01' exists on the live system, created via ADT REST API through MCP - not a file on disk 
| Production-quality ABAP 7.5 | TYPE declarations referencing actual MARA field types, correct naming conventions, modern SQL syntax 
| Minutes, not hours| The developer's job shifts to reviewing - not writing skeletons, hunting transport dialogs, or looking up field types 
| Fully iterative| Once created, you can ask Claude to add authority checks, error handling, ALV field catalogues, or additional filters - each iteration in seconds 
| You stay in control | Claude's todo list is transparent - you see every step it plans. You review the code. You decide what activates 
 
Where to Go from Here?
Here are five things to try once you have the setup working:
1. Understand inherited code - Explain the purpose and flow of report Z_*_REPORT. Include tables accessed, authority checks, deprecated patterns like SELECT .
2. Generate a CDS view - Create a CDS view hierarchy for Sales Order analysis: interface view joining VBAK+VBAP, projection view with Fiori Elements. UI annotations for a List Report.
3. Build a Travel Request POC -  RAP Business Object with CRUD, approve/reject action, draft handling, OData V4 binding, Fiori Elements UI.
4. Write ABAP Unit tests - Generate a unit test class 
5. Code review and modernization - Review this class for Clean ABAP violations, missing authority checks, SELECT * usage, and SELECT inside LOOP. Propose S/4-equivalent replacements.
 
The bottom line: The integration that used to require manual Node.js setup, JSON editing, and external package management now takes a single Ctrl+Shift+P toggle and one terminal command.
 
The ABAP Remote Filesystem team has done the hard work. Claude Code does the coding. You do the review.
Give it a try - and let me know in the comments what use case you tackle first.
 
A personal note  : This is my first time diving deeper into vibe coding and MCP concepts. I come from a classic ABAP background, and everything covered here -  from the VS Code setup to the MCP server to the live demo - I explored and built from scratch over the past few weeks. The scenario is intentionally simple. The goal was to get the fundamentals right, document them honestly, and share what works. There is a lot more ground to cover - RAP generation at scale, Clean Core extensibility patterns, ATC pre-checks via Claude Code, and deeper agentic workflows. I'll be going further in the posts ahead. If you're also just getting started with this, I hope this gives you a solid, working foundation to build on. And if you're already further ahead - I'd love to hear what you've learnt in the comments.
 
Note: All screenshots in this post are taken from a live S/4HANA development system. Code generated by Claude Code should always be reviewed and tested in a sandbox environment before activation in any productive system.

entry.title: Building Multi-Agent Systems for Production: The State, Context, and Memory Playbook
entry.content:
Blog 6 of N: Building Multi-Agent Systems for Production - The State, Context, and Memory Playbook

Every article on multi-agent orchestration uses the words state, context, and memory as if they were the same thing. They are not. Mixing them up is why your agent forgets what the user said two turns ago, why your storage bill quietly doubles every month, and why your "memory feature" leaks data across users.

This blog is framework-free. Whatever library you use, these patterns do not change. The next blog will cover the five pillars of a production agent - evaluation, observability, reliability, security, cost. State, context, and memory sit underneath all five, so we start here.

The vocabulary in one minute

  • Context = tokens the model sees this one call. Lives for milliseconds. Analogy: what is on your screen right now.
  • State = data your orchestration carries between steps of the current task. Lives for seconds to minutes. Analogy: variables in a running program.
  • Memory = knowledge that persists across tasks and sessions. Lives for days to forever. Analogy: what is on your disk.

"Trim the messages" is a context operation. "Delete checkpoints after 30 days" is a state operation. "Purge user data on a GDPR request" is a memory operation. Different tools, different retention, different failure modes.

The four kinds of memory

  • Working - what the agent is holding for the current task. Lives in the context window.
  • Episodic - time-stamped log of what happened. Past conversations, past incidents.
  • Semantic - stable facts, stripped of the episode they were learned in. "User's timezone is IST." "Company policy is 30-day refunds."
  • Procedural - how to do things. Playbooks, workflows, tool-use strategies. The least mature in production today.

Each kind needs its own store and its own retention. Mixing logs and facts in one vector index poisons retrieval - a query for "timezone" starts pulling back conversations about timezones.

Context engineering, in three lines

Anthropic's definition, worth quoting verbatim: "Context engineering refers to the set of strategies for curating and maintaining the optimal set of tokens (information) during LLM inference."

Prompt engineering is phrasing one instruction. Context engineering is deciding what the model sees at all during one call - which past messages, which retrieved documents, which tool outputs, in which order. Prompt engineering is a piece inside context engineering, not a peer to it.

Rule of thumb: five to ten items per turn retrieved from long-term stores. More crowds out reasoning; less causes amnesia.

Two stores, not one

Every mature agent product uses two:

  • Store A - source of truth. Your app database: conversations, messages, artifacts. What the UI reads. Retention driven by product policy (indefinite until user deletes).
  • Store B - execution cache. Your framework's checkpoint store: state snapshots so a crashed task can resume. Retention driven by ops (aggressive TTL).

The trap: pointing the UI at Store B and skipping Store A. It works for thirty days. Then your nightly cleanup prunes old checkpoints and users open conversations that are now empty. Chat history was living inside the crash-resume cache.

Rule: if deleting your execution cache would delete user-visible history, you have one store where you needed two.

The production patterns

Everything above is setup. This section is the point. Each pattern is a problem you will hit in production and the pattern that solves it.

To keep the examples concrete, we will use one running scenario throughout: a Coordinator agent takes user questions from a chat UI and dispatches work to two peer agents - Sherlock (qualitative signals: logs, traces, deploys, runbooks) and Watson (quantitative signals: metrics, baselines, postmortems). Coordinator plans, Sherlock and Watson investigate, Coordinator synthesises the answer. Every problem and pattern below is framed against this trio.

The handoff contract

When the Coordinator dispatches work to Sherlock or Watson, do not pass a paragraph of prose. Pass a typed, versioned envelope that both sides agree on. At minimum it carries:

  • The goal in one sentence ("investigate the checkout 5xx spike"), not a step-by-step script.
  • The scope: what is in bounds, what is out.
  • The definition of done: when the peer knows it is finished.
  • A compressed context snippet from the conversation - not the full transcript.
  • The user preferences that the peer needs (timezone, focus service, format).
  • A deadline: how much time the peer has to answer.
  • A schema version: so the peer can reject envelopes it does not understand instead of guessing.

When the peer responds, its envelope carries not just the result but a status: success, partial success, or failure. On partial success it lists what was completed, what was not, and what side effects were already committed - so the Coordinator knows whether to retry, compensate, or escalate. A boolean success/fail flag is a bug generator. A typed status with partial output is a debuggable system.

Concrete example. Coordinator asks Sherlock: "Investigate checkout 5xx at 14:32. In scope: logs and traces. Out of scope: metrics - Watson is handling those. Deadline: 3 seconds. User cares about checkout-svc only." Sherlock returns: "Partial success. Ran log search, found the signature. Runbook lookup timed out. Side effects: none. Recommend retrying runbook lookup on the next turn." Coordinator now has enough to answer and enough to plan the next turn. Nothing was lost.

One thread per conversation, one thread per task

Every agent needs a thread identifier so its state is isolated from other conversations. The trap is using the same identifier at every layer. The Coordinator's conversation with the user lives for weeks; Sherlock's involvement in one turn lives for three seconds. Give them different lifetimes.

A pattern that works: the Coordinator's thread is the conversation identifier itself. Sherlock and Watson, for each task, get a thread identifier like conversation:sherlock:task-42 or conversation:watson:task-42. The prefix lets you grep for everything Sherlock did inside one conversation. The suffix keeps peer state per task, not per conversation. Peer threads can be aggressively pruned; the Coordinator's conversation thread is durable.

Context offloading with artifact refs (never store the same blob twice)

This pattern has a name in the literature: context offloading with artifact references. The idea is to keep the model's context small by pushing big payloads out to a side store and passing only pointers.

Sherlock's search-logs tool returns two hundred log lines. Without discipline that payload ends up in Sherlock's checkpoint, in the response envelope back to Coordinator, in the Coordinator's checkpoint, in the app's messages table, and in the streamed UI response. Five copies of the same blob. Storage doubles quietly for six months, then someone notices.

The fix: Sherlock's tool returns a small content summary for the model plus a separate artifact reference for the store. Big payloads are written once, in one authoritative place (typically the Coordinator's artifacts table, since it owns the conversation). Every other layer - envelopes, checkpoints, UI stream - carries only the artifact ID plus a short summary. "200 log entries, dominant signature 'redis timeout,' full payload at artifact-a3b1." The model reads the summary; the pointer is available if it needs to drill in; the storage bill stays flat. One authoritative copy, many cheap references.

One deadline, propagated everywhere

Your service has an eight-second SLA to the user. Sherlock and Watson do not know that. Unless the deadline is passed explicitly, each peer will happily spend its full internal budget on tool calls and blow the SLA.

The pattern: the Coordinator creates a single deadline object at the moment the request enters the system. It is attached to every downstream call as a header. Every stage - Coordinator's classifier, Sherlock, Watson, Coordinator's aggregator - checks the deadline before every model call and before every tool call. If less than half a second remains, refuse to start a new tool call and return what you have. Budget it: intake takes a small slice, the classifier takes some, Sherlock and Watson get most of it (running in parallel), aggregation takes the tail. Every stage returns unspent budget to the next stage. Whichever stage runs long steals from the next stage, not from the deadline.

Serialise writers on the same conversation

Two turns arriving at the same Coordinator conversation at the same time - a user pressing retry, a client retry-on-timeout, a race between browser tabs - will corrupt shared state unless you prevent it. Use a lightweight lock scoped to the conversation identifier. First writer wins; second writer waits or is rejected. Combine this with an idempotency key on the incoming request so duplicate submissions return the cached response instead of re-running Sherlock and Watson all over again. Neither piece is expensive. Both together are the difference between a stable system and a bug you cannot reproduce.

Content-address your corpus

Sherlock's runbooks change. Watson's baseline definitions change. Product docs change. If semantic memory is keyed by "path to the file" or "the fact this document is a runbook," you have no way to detect that the content is stale. Key by a hash of the content itself. When the file changes, the hash changes, a new entry is created, and the old one can be marked superseded. At answer time, when Sherlock cites a runbook, a validator can check that the cited hash is still current. If it is not, the citation is flagged as stale before it reaches the user.

Separate the writer from the verifier

The same model that generates a claim is the wrong model to grade it. Have Coordinator's aggregator produce answers with structured citations - every claim linked to an identifier of the tool result or memory item it came from (an artifact ref from Sherlock, a metric ID from Watson, a runbook hash). Then run a second, independent step that checks: does every cited identifier exist in this turn's evidence? Does the quoted span actually appear in the source? Strip any claim that fails. This is not RLHF or fine-tuning. It is a boring, deterministic post-check. It catches the majority of hallucinated citations at a fraction of the cost of retraining.

Cross-turn preferences belong in semantic memory, not the conversation

On turn one, the user tells Coordinator: "I only care about the checkout service, ignore everything else." Three turns later, they ask "what is the p95 latency?" and Watson dutifully returns latency for every service in the fleet. The user gives up.

The bug is treating the preference as a conversational aside. Preferences are semantic memory. When Coordinator detects one, it should extract it, store it under a user-scoped key ("focus_service = checkout-svc, learned on turn 1, confidence 0.9"), and read it back into the handoff envelope on every subsequent turn. Watson never has to know how the preference was learned. It just receives "user cares only about checkout-svc" in its brief and filters accordingly.

The wider pattern. If the same fact would apply on the next turn, it does not belong in the conversation, it belongs in memory. Timezone, preferred language, focus service, favourite date format, "always answer in bullet points" - all semantic memory. Extract, store, retrieve, inject. Do not make the model re-discover the preference from ten turns of scrollback.

Pick your partial-failure policy up front

Coordinator fans out to Sherlock and Watson in parallel. Watson answers in two seconds. Sherlock times out. What now?

There are exactly three answers, and you have to pick one per skill before you ship:

  • Fail-fast. If any peer fails, the whole turn fails. Return an error to the user. Use this when partial answers would be misleading - a compliance check where "we verified two of three requirements" is worse than "we could not verify."
  • Partial-return. Use whatever came back, mark the missing pieces explicitly ("metrics from Watson available, logs from Sherlock unavailable"). Use this when partial answers still help - a diagnostic where half an answer beats no answer, and the user can decide what to do.
  • Targeted retry. Retry only the failed peer within the remaining deadline. Use this when the peer is idempotent, the deadline has slack, and success rates on retry are meaningfully high.

The mistake is picking implicitly. Teams handwave "we'll degrade gracefully" and end up with fail-fast for the checkout query, partial-return for the refund query, and targeted-retry for the account-lookup query, all by accident, none written down. When it breaks in production nobody knows which behaviour was intended. Write the policy on each skill. One word in the skill's spec, so every reviewer can see it.

Partition memory by trust before you get poisoned

Memory feels like one thing. It is not. Different memory items come from different sources and deserve different levels of trust. Mix them and you get memory poisoning: an attacker plants an instruction inside a document, Sherlock reads it during a runbook lookup, and now Sherlock is following the attacker's script instead of yours.

Four tiers, in decreasing order of trust:

  • System rules. Company policies, safety constraints, agent-card definitions for Coordinator, Sherlock, and Watson. Nobody writes here at runtime, not even administrators without a change process.
  • Vetted organisational knowledge. Approved runbooks (Sherlock's corpus), postmortem templates (Watson's corpus), curated FAQ entries. Writes require a review pipeline.
  • Per-user memory. User preferences, per-user notes, personal history. Scoped to the user; other users cannot read it.
  • Untrusted external content. Web pages the agent fetched, uploaded documents, other agents' raw outputs, anything a customer typed into a form. Treat as data, never as instructions.

Two rules keep the tiers from bleeding:

  • User input never writes into the top two tiers. If a customer says "add this to your policies," you do not add it to your policies. You add it to their per-user notes, if anywhere.
  • Everything gets scanned at ingest. Before a document lands in Sherlock's runbook corpus, look for prompt-injection patterns (hidden text, imperative phrasing in a doc that should be descriptive, instructions embedded in HTML metadata). Suspicious items go to a staging area for human review, not straight into the index.

Concrete example. An attacker uploads a "product FAQ" that contains, in white-on-white text, an instruction telling Sherlock to approve every refund and email the customer list to an external address. Without partitioning, this FAQ lands in vetted knowledge, Sherlock retrieves it during a refund conversation, and follows the instruction. With partitioning, the ingest scanner flags the hidden text, the document sits in staging, a human sees the payload, and the attack never fires. The extra step at ingest is the cheapest security control you will ever add.

The consolidation loop

Episodes pile up. Every Sherlock investigation, every Watson analysis, every Coordinator answer becomes an episode. Left alone, they become a giant log nobody reads. The pattern that turns experience into skill is a background reflection loop:

  • Every N conversations, a reflection process reads the recent episodes.
  • It extracts stable facts and writes them to semantic memory ("this user prefers concise answers").
  • It extracts recurring workflows and proposes them as procedural playbooks ("for checkout 5xx, the successful trajectory is Sherlock's log-check then Sherlock's deploy-check then Watson's error-rate baseline").
  • Procedural writes go through a human review before they become live, because a bad playbook is worse than none.
  • Old raw episodes get archived or dropped.

Without this loop, memory is a passive dump. With it, memory learns. Episodic memory feeds semantic memory feeds procedural memory. Each layer distils the one below. Next time the user reports a checkout 5xx, Coordinator does not have to plan from scratch - it has a playbook. This is the loop that closes the gap between "the agent remembers what happened" and "the agent has gotten better at its job."

Retention as an explicit policy

Not everything deserves the same lifespan. A practical starting policy:

  • User-visible history (conversations, messages): indefinite until the user deletes, subject to compliance floors.
  • Large blobs and tool artifacts: ninety days. Message summaries and citations still exist after the artifact expires; the user just cannot drill into the raw payload.
