  • Executives may receive high-level strategic insights.

  • Operations teams may interact with supply chain or operational AI models.

Rather than exposing a single AI model to every user, future implementations could leverage ContextWallet as a session-aware context layer capable of managing AI routing and authorization.

A session identifier or secure context token could be maintained alongside business context, allowing applications to determine:

  • Which AI model should receive a request.

  • Which data domains are accessible.

  • Which analytical capabilities are available.

  • Which business entities a user is authorized to view.

In this scenario, ContextWallet would not simply store dashboard filters but would become part of a broader AI governance framework.

A simplified future architecture could look like this:

User
  │
  ▼
SAP Analytics Cloud
  │
  ▼
ContextWallet
  │
  ├── Business Context
  ├── Session Context
  ├── Security Context
  └── User Role Context
  │
  ▼
AI Routing Layer
  │
  ├── Finance AI Assistant
  ├── FP&A Copilot
  ├── Executive Insights Model
  └── Operations Intelligence Agent

Such an approach would allow organizations to provide tailored AI experiences while maintaining security, compliance, and governance requirements.

Although these capabilities are currently part of the long-term vision rather than the current implementation, they demonstrate how Context Engineering can evolve beyond dashboard state management and become a foundational layer for enterprise AI ecosystems.

More Than a Storage Widget

While ContextWallet currently provides capabilities such as context storage, retrieval, snapshot management, and AI payload generation, its long-term vision extends far beyond traditional state management.

At its core, ContextWallet represents an exploration of Context Engineering within SAP Analytics Cloud—a concept that I believe will become increasingly important as organizations integrate AI into their analytics ecosystems.

Today, business context is often fragmented across filters, input controls, application variables, and user selections. Human users can naturally understand this context because it is visible on the dashboard. AI systems, however, typically receive only the final question, without access to the business environment in which that question was asked.

ContextWallet attempts to bridge this gap by establishing a centralized context layer that can be shared across dashboards, applications, users, and AI services.

The objective is not simply to remember values.

The objective is to create a context-aware analytical environment where dashboards, business processes, and AI systems operate using a common understanding of the user's current business scenario.

Looking ahead, the same architecture could evolve beyond dashboard context management and become part of a broader AI governance and orchestration framework.

Future implementations could leverage context, session identifiers, role information, and security metadata to route requests to specialized AI models based on business function and user authorization.

For example:

  • Finance teams could interact with financial planning copilots.

  • FP&A users could access forecasting and variance analysis assistants.

  • Executives could receive strategic insight generation models.

  • Operations teams could utilize supply-chain-specific AI agents.

In such an architecture, ContextWallet would not only manage business context but also contribute to AI session management, security enforcement, model routing, and role-based AI experiences.

In other words, the future vision is not merely a smarter dashboard.

It is the creation of a shared context layer that enables analytics applications and AI systems to collaborate more effectively, securely, and intelligently.

The next section explores the technical architecture behind ContextWallet and demonstrates how it can act as an intermediary layer between SAP Analytics Cloud and AI platforms such as Databricks Genie.

Architecture and Technical Design

 

ContextWallet Architecture

 

 

After establishing the concept of Context Engineering, the next step was designing a practical implementation that could operate within SAP Analytics Cloud while remaining flexible enough to support future AI integrations.

The primary design objective was to create a lightweight, reusable context layer that could sit between dashboard interactions and AI systems.

Rather than allowing filters, selections, and application state to remain scattered throughout the application, ContextWallet centralizes this information into a structured context object that can be accessed by both SAC components and external services.

High-Level Architecture

At a high level, ContextWallet acts as an intermediary layer between user interactions and AI systems.

The workflow begins when users interact with dashboard components such as:

  • Input Controls

  • Dropdowns

  • Filters

  • Tables

  • Application Variables

  • Planning Selections

These interactions generate business context that is relevant to the current analytical session.

Instead of allowing each component to manage its own state independently, ContextWallet collects and stores this information in a centralized context repository.

The stored context can then be consumed by:

  • SAC Application Scripts

  • Dashboard Components

  • Snapshot Management Functions

  • AI Payload Builders

  • External AI Platforms such as Databricks Genie

This creates a single source of truth for business context throughout the application.

Context Lifecycle

The architecture follows a simple but effective lifecycle:

Step 1: Context Capture

User selections are captured from dashboard components and stored in ContextWallet.

For example:

ContextWallet_1.setContext({
    CompanyCode: "1000",
    FiscalYear: "2025",
    Version: "Actual",
    Currency: "USD"
});

At this stage, ContextWallet becomes aware of the user's analytical environment.

Step 2: Context Persistence

Once stored, the context remains available throughout the application lifecycle.

This enables:

  • Cross-page navigation

  • Multi-step workflows

  • Dashboard state retention

  • Consistent AI interactions

Instead of repeatedly requesting filter information from multiple controls, applications can retrieve the required context directly from ContextWallet.

Step 3: Context Enrichment

As users continue interacting with the dashboard, additional information can be added to the context.

For example:

ContextWallet_1.mergeContext({
    Entity: "EU_Corp",
    AccountType: "Revenue"
});

The context evolves alongside the user's analytical journey.

Step 4: Context Consumption

When an AI interaction occurs, ContextWallet combines the user's question with the active business context.

For example:

var payload =
ContextWallet_1.getAIPayload(
    "Why is revenue decreasing?"
);

Result:

{
  "question": "Why is revenue decreasing?",
  "context": {
    "CompanyCode": "1000",
    "FiscalYear": "2025",
    "Currency": "USD",
    "Entity": "EU_Corp",
    "AccountType": "Revenue"
  }
}

Rather than receiving an isolated question, the AI receives both the question and the business scenario in which it was asked.

Snapshot Engine

One of the core architectural components of ContextWallet is the Snapshot Engine.

Snapshots allow complete analytical states to be preserved and restored.

For example, a finance team conducting a month-end review may save the current context as:

ContextWallet_1.saveSnapshot(
    "MonthEndReview"
);

This snapshot captures the entire business context at that point in time.

Later, the same context can be restored using:

ContextWallet_1.loadSnapshot(
    "MonthEndReview"
);

This capability provides reproducibility for analytical workflows and establishes the foundation for future context comparison and AI memory scenarios.

Preparing for AI Integration

The architecture was intentionally designed with AI integrations in mind.

Traditional AI implementations typically receive only the user's prompt.

ContextWallet introduces an additional layer:

Question
+
Business Context
+
Session Context
+
Application State

This combination allows future AI systems to generate responses that are aware of:

  • Current dashboard filters

  • Active reporting periods

  • Business entities

  • User workflow state

  • Saved analytical scenarios

In essence, ContextWallet transforms dashboard interactions into structured knowledge that can be consumed by AI systems.

Extending the Architecture

Although the current implementation focuses on context management, the architecture has been designed to support future enhancements such as:

  • Context history tracking

  • Session management

  • AI routing

  • Role-based AI experiences

  • Context-aware security controls

  • Multi-agent orchestration

These capabilities could enable organizations to move beyond isolated AI assistants and toward fully context-aware analytics ecosystems.

The next section demonstrates how this architecture can be applied to a practical use case by integrating SAP Analytics Cloud with Databricks Genie through a shared context layer.

SAC + Databricks Genie: A Practical Context Engineering Use Case

The concept of Context Engineering becomes most valuable when we examine how SAP Analytics Cloud can interact with AI-powered analytics platforms such as Databricks Genie.

Databricks Genie enables users to ask business questions in natural language and receive insights directly from enterprise data. This significantly lowers the barrier to analytics by allowing business users to interact with data conversationally instead of relying solely on dashboards and reports.

 

 

 

However, when integrating SAC with Genie, a challenge immediately emerges.

The Context Gap

Consider a financial analyst working inside a SAC dashboard.

The analyst has already configured the dashboard using several business filters:

  • Company Code = 1000

  • Fiscal Year = 2025

  • Version = Actual

  • Currency = USD

  • Entity = EU_Corp

  • Account Type = Revenue

After reviewing the dashboard, the analyst asks:

Why is revenue lower than last year?

While the question appears complete from the user's perspective, it lacks the surrounding business context that exists within the dashboard.

Without access to the dashboard state, Genie may not know:

  • Which company is being analyzed

  • Which fiscal year is active

  • Which currency is being used

  • Which reporting version is selected

  • Which business entity is in focus

As a result, the AI system may generate a generic response or require additional clarification from the user.

This creates friction in the analytical workflow because users are forced to repeatedly provide information that already exists within the dashboard.

Context-Aware AI Interactions

This is where ContextWallet introduces a different approach.

Instead of sending only the user's question to Genie, ContextWallet can package both the question and the active dashboard context into a structured payload.

For example:

{
  "question": "Why is revenue lower than last year?",
  "context": {
    "CompanyCode": "1000",
    "FiscalYear": "2025",
    "Version": "Actual",
    "Currency": "USD",
    "Entity": "EU_Corp",
    "AccountType": "Revenue"
  }
}

From an AI perspective, this additional information is extremely valuable.

The question is no longer interpreted in isolation.

It is interpreted within the business scenario that the user is currently analyzing.

This allows AI systems to generate more focused, relevant, and actionable responses.

Improving User Experience

One of the most significant advantages of this approach is the reduction of repetitive user interactions.

Without Context Engineering:

User:
Why is revenue decreasing?

AI:
Which company?

User:
Company Code 1000.

AI:
Which fiscal year?

User:
2025.

AI:
Which reporting version?

User:
Actual.

With Context Engineering:

User:
Why is revenue decreasing?

AI:
Analyzes Company Code 1000,
Fiscal Year 2025,
Version Actual,
and provides a contextualized answer.

The user interacts naturally while the application provides the necessary context behind the scenes.

Beyond Questions and Answers

The potential value of ContextWallet extends beyond conversational analytics.

Future integrations could enable AI systems to access:

  • Current dashboard state

  • Saved snapshots

  • Historical analytical scenarios

  • User workflow context

  • Session metadata

  • Role information

This would allow AI systems to understand not only the current question but also the broader analytical journey of the user.

For example:

What changed since the Month-End Review?

Instead of treating this as a generic question, the AI could compare the current dashboard context with a previously saved snapshot and generate insights based on the differences.

Similarly, planning teams could compare forecast versions, executive teams could analyze strategic review snapshots, and finance teams could revisit previous reporting scenarios.

From Dashboards to Context-Aware Analytics

Traditional dashboards excel at presenting information.

AI systems excel at interpreting information.

However, without a shared context layer, these systems often operate independently.

ContextWallet attempts to bridge this gap by establishing a common context model that can be understood by both SAP Analytics Cloud and external AI platforms.

In this architecture, the dashboard becomes the source of business context, ContextWallet becomes the context management layer, and Databricks Genie becomes the intelligence layer.

Together, they create a foundation for a new generation of context-aware analytics applications where users can move seamlessly between dashboards, business workflows, and conversational AI experiences.

Future Vision: From Context Management to AI Orchestration

 

 

 

 

The current implementation of ContextWallet focuses on a relatively simple objective: capturing, preserving, and sharing business context within SAP Analytics Cloud.

However, while building and experimenting with the framework, it became apparent that the underlying concept could extend far beyond dashboard state management.

As organizations continue integrating AI into enterprise analytics, a new challenge emerges:

How do we manage, govern, and orchestrate multiple AI systems while maintaining security, context, and business relevance?

This is where the future vision of ContextWallet begins.

Beyond Dashboard Context

Today, ContextWallet primarily stores:

  • Dashboard filters

  • User selections

  • Application state

  • Analytical snapshots

  • AI payload context

In the future, the same architecture could be extended to manage additional layers of information such as:

  • Session Context

  • User Context

  • Security Context

  • Role Context

  • AI Routing Metadata

This would allow ContextWallet to evolve from a context repository into a centralized context orchestration layer.

AI Session Management

One of the most interesting future applications involves AI session management.

Enterprise AI interactions are rarely isolated events.

Users often engage in a sequence of related analytical conversations, building upon previous questions and insights.

For example:

Question 1:
Why is revenue decreasing?

Question 2:
Which products contributed most?

Question 3:
How does this compare with last quarter?

These questions are connected.

Without session awareness, each interaction must be treated independently.

With session-aware context management, AI systems can maintain continuity across analytical conversations and provide more relevant responses.

Future versions of ContextWallet could store session identifiers and maintain conversational context throughout the user's analytical journey.

Role-Based AI Experiences

Not every user requires access to the same AI capabilities.

A finance analyst, a controller, a planner, and an executive often have very different responsibilities and information requirements.

For example:

Finance Analyst

  • Variance Analysis

  • Financial Reporting

  • Cost Analysis

FP&A Team

  • Forecasting

  • Budget Planning

  • Scenario Simulation

Executive Leadership

  • Strategic Insights

  • KPI Summaries

  • Business Performance Reviews

Rather than exposing a single AI assistant to every user, future implementations could dynamically route requests to specialized AI systems based on role and authorization.

This would enable organizations to create tailored AI experiences while maintaining governance and security controls.

AI Routing and Model Selection

As AI ecosystems mature, organizations are unlikely to rely on a single model.

Instead, different AI systems may be optimized for different tasks.

For example:

Financial Analysis Model
        │
Forecasting Model
        │
Planning Copilot
        │
Executive Insights Assistant
        │
Operational Intelligence Agent

Future versions of ContextWallet could act as an intelligent routing layer.

Based on context, user role, and session metadata, requests could automatically be directed to the most appropriate AI system.

This would allow users to interact with a unified interface while the platform manages the complexity behind the scenes.

Security and Governance

Enterprise AI adoption introduces important governance requirements.

Organizations must ensure that users only access information relevant to their responsibilities.

ContextWallet could potentially support this through:

  • Role-aware context management

  • Secure session identifiers

  • Access-control metadata

  • AI authorization rules

For example, an executive assistant might receive organization-wide insights, while a finance analyst receives only information related to their assigned entities.

This approach would help ensure that AI interactions remain aligned with existing security and compliance frameworks.

Towards a Context-Aware Analytics Ecosystem

The long-term vision is not simply a smarter dashboard.

The vision is a context-aware analytics ecosystem where dashboards, applications, business processes, and AI systems operate using a shared understanding of the business environment.

In such an ecosystem:

  • Dashboards generate context.

  • ContextWallet manages context.

  • AI systems consume context.

  • Business users receive contextualized insights.

Instead of repeatedly explaining the business scenario to every application and AI assistant, context becomes a reusable enterprise asset.

Getting Started with ContextWallet

For readers interested in experimenting with ContextWallet, the project is available as an open-source SAP Analytics Cloud Custom Widget.

GitHub Repository

Source Code:

https://github.com/sampurno417/ContextWallet

The repository contains:

  • ContextWallet Widget Source Code

  • Widget Manifest (JSON)

  • Production Bundle

  • Documentation

  • Example Usage Patterns

Installation

Step 1: Download the Widget Manifest

Open the repository and download the latest ContextWallet JSON manifest.

Step 2: Import the Widget into SAP Analytics Cloud

Navigate to:

Analytics Designer
→ Custom Widgets
→ Add Custom Widget
→ Upload JSON Manifest

Once imported, ContextWallet becomes available within your SAC application.

Step 3: Add ContextWallet to the Application

Drag and drop the widget onto any page.

The widget itself does not need to be visible to end users and can be hidden after being added to the application.

ContextWallet is primarily designed as a backend utility component accessed through Analytics Designer scripting.

Basic Usage

Store Context

ContextWallet_1.setContext({
    CompanyCode: "1000",
    FiscalYear: "2025",
    Currency: "USD"
});

Retrieve Context

var context =
ContextWallet_1.getContext();

console.log(context);

Add Additional Fields

ContextWallet_1.addContextField(
    "Entity",
    "EU_Corp"
);

Merge Context

ContextWallet_1.mergeContext({
    AccountType: "Revenue"
});

Working with Snapshots

Save the current dashboard state:

ContextWallet_1.saveSnapshot(
    "MonthEndReview"
);

Retrieve available snapshots:

var snapshots =
ContextWallet_1.getSnapshots();

Restore a snapshot:

ContextWallet_1.loadSnapshot(
    "MonthEndReview"
);

Preparing AI Payloads

ContextWallet can generate structured payloads suitable for AI integrations.

var payload =
ContextWallet_1.getAIPayload(
    "Why is revenue decreasing?"
);

console.log(payload);

Example output:

{
  "question": "Why is revenue decreasing?",
  "context": {
    "CompanyCode": "1000",
    "FiscalYear": "2025",
    "Currency": "USD",
    "Entity": "EU_Corp"
  }
}

This payload can be passed to external AI systems such as Databricks Genie, custom LLM applications, or enterprise copilots.

Current Status

ContextWallet is currently an experimental framework focused on exploring Context Engineering concepts within SAP Analytics Cloud.

The project continues to evolve, with future enhancements planned around:

  • AI Session Management

  • Role-Based AI Routing

  • Security Context

  • Context History

  • Multi-Agent AI Integrations

  • Databricks Genie Connectivity

Feedback, suggestions, and collaboration ideas are always welcome.

Conclusion

As Generative AI becomes increasingly embedded within enterprise analytics platforms, context will play a critical role in determining the quality, relevance, and trustworthiness of AI-generated insights.

ContextWallet began as an experiment in solving a practical problem within SAP Analytics Cloud: preserving and sharing dashboard context.

Along the way, it revealed a broader architectural concept that I refer to as Context Engineering.

While the current implementation focuses on context management, snapshots, and AI payload generation, the underlying idea has the potential to evolve into a foundational layer for future AI-enabled analytics applications.

The journey is still at an early stage, but one thing is becoming increasingly clear:

In the era of enterprise AI, understanding the question is important.

Understanding the context behind the question is essential.

This project was developed as part of my exploration into SAP Analytics Cloud, AI-assisted analytics, and Databricks Genie integrations. I hope it encourages further discussion around Context Engineering and the role of context-aware architectures in the future of enterprise analytics.

entry.title: Multi-System Joule Support for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
entry.content:
Introduction

In today's enterprise landscape, organizations rarely operate with just one SAP S/4HANA system. Whether driven by complex organizational structures, regional requirements, or company acquisitions, businesses typically maintain multiple S/4HANA Cloud systems across their landscape. Each system serves specific purposes: perhaps one handles finance for EMEA, another manages operations in North America, and yet another supports a recently acquired subsidiary.

Until recently, Joule faced a significant limitation: it could only connect to a single SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud system. This meant that users working across multiple systems couldn't leverage Joule's AI capabilities uniformly across their entire landscape. Organizations found themselves in a challenging position: either create multiple Joule instances (one for each S/4HANA system) or accept limited coverage of their system landscape.
The good news? Joule now supports multi-system connectivity for both SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition (SAP Help Documentation). With this enhancement, you can configure one Joule instance to connect to multiple S/4HANA systems, providing your users with a unified, intelligent assistant experience across their entire landscape.
 

What Multi-System Support Enables

With multi-system Joule, you can:
Unified Experience: One Joule instance serves all your S/4HANA Private Cloud systems
Intelligent System Selection: Joule automatically determines the right system based on user prompts and authorizations
Cross-Landscape Coverage: Users access scenarios across different systems in a single conversation
Simplified Administration: Manage one Joule instance instead of multiple isolated instances

This blog post will guide you through the step-by-step process of activating multi-system support for Private
Cloud Edition, complete with best practices to optimize your setup and ensure your users get the most out of
this powerful capability.
 

Architecture Overview

The architecture shows how a single Joule instance connects to multiple SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud systems through SAP Build Work Zone and BTP destinations, enabling unified access across your entire system landscape.

How Multi-System Joule Works

Multi-system Joule fundamentally changes how the digital assistant interacts with your system landscape:

System Awareness
Joule capabilities become system-aware, meaning they're visible and available based on specific conditions, primarily user authorizations and system access. When a user opens Joule, they see scenarios relevant to the systems they're authorized to execute.

Intelligent System Selection
When you submit a prompt to Joule, the AI analyzes several factors to determine the appropriate target system:
  1. Your user authorizations across different systems
  2. System descriptions and characteristics configured in the Joule Admin Center
  3. Keywords and context within your prompt
  4. Previously selected systems within the current conversation
Single-System Execution
While Joule can connect to multiple systems, each individual scenario executes in exactly one system at a time. This ensures data consistency and clear transactional boundaries. However, you can run multiple scenarios across different systems within the same conversation.

Disambiguation Flow
If Joule can't confidently determine the target system from your prompt, it will ask you to select the appropriate system. Your selection is remembered for the conversation context, reducing the need for repeated clarifications.
 

Prerequisites

Before you begin setting up multi-system support, ensure you have the following in place:
  • Existing Joule Setup: Joule must already be configured and operational following the Joule Onboarding Guide
  • First S/4HANA System / Client: At least one SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud system already integrated with Joule via the standard integration process
  • Other S/4HANA Systems / Clients: Prepare the integration of other systems / clients by following the integration steps outlined in the Integration Guide for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Please Skip number 8 (Run the Joule Booster) and the creation of the SSO Destination (part of step 9) as these steps will be covered during this blog.
  • Admin Roles: Joule Tenant Admin roles assigned to your user for accessing the Joule Admin Center
  • BTP Access: Administrator access to your SAP BTP Cockpit for destination management
  • SAP Build Work Zone Access: Access to SAP Build Work Zone (standard or advanced edition) for managing content providers and channels

Step-by-Step Activation Process

Step 1: Configure BTP Destinations

The foundation of multi-system Joule is proper BTP destination configuration. Each S/4HANA system requires its own set of destinations, and since BTP doesn't allow duplicate destination names, you must use a clear naming convention.

Understanding the Naming Convention

Pattern: {DESTINATION_BASE_NAME}_{SYSTEM_ID}{SYSTEM_CLIENT}
Examples:
Original default: S4HANA_PCE_SSO
For system XXX123: S4HANA_PCE_SSO_XXX123
For system YYY789: S4HANA_PCE_SSO_YYY789

This naming pattern ensures destinations are:
Unique: Prevents naming conflicts in BTP
Identifiable: Administrators can immediately see which system a destination serves
Consistent: Follows a predictable pattern across all systems
 
! Make sure {SYSTEM_ID}does not include any special characters (./,;:\#) or white spaces !

Rename Your Existing Default Destination

  1. Navigate to your SAP BTP Cockpit → Your Subaccount → Connectivity → Destinations
  2. Locate the default destination created during your initial Joule setup: S4HANA_PCE_SSO
  3. Click the destination name to open its configuration
  4. Click Edit
  5. Update the Name field to include your system ID as a suffix
  6. Example: If your system is XXX with client 123, rename to S4HANA_PCE_SSO_XXX123
  7. Leave all other configuration settings unchanged
  8. Click Save
Screenshot of the new Destination Name

Create Destinations for Additional Systems

For each additional S/4HANA Private Cloud system you want to connect:
  1. Follow the standard destination configuration process for S/4HANA Private Cloud integration
  2. Apply the naming convention with the new system's ID
  3. Example: S4HANA_PCE_SSO_YYY789
  4. Configure all required destination properties (URL, authentication, etc.) to point to the new system
PropertyDescription/Value
NameS4HANA_PCE_SSO_{SYSTEM_ID}{SYSTEM_CLIENT}
TypeHTTP
DescriptionEnter a description.
URLThe URL of the Virtual Host of your Cloud Connector: 
https://<host>:<port>
Proxy TypeOnPremise
AuthenticationPrincipal Propagation
sap-client (additional property)
The client number of the ABAP system.
 
Critical Warning: Do NOT re-run the Joule Booster for additional systems. The Booster should only be used for initial Joule setup. For multi-system configuration, manually create destinations following the documented process.
 

Step 2: Include Systems in Joule Formation

Once your BTP destinations are configured, you need to register the additional S/4HANA systems in the Joule Formation.

Add Systems to Formation

  1. Navigate to your SAP BTP Cockpit → Your Global Account Overview → System Landscape: Formations
  2. Locate your Joule Formation
  3. In the Formation view, click the Include Systems button
  4. The "Include Systems in Formation" wizard opens

Configure Integration Details

On the Integration Details screen:
  1. From SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition [SYSTEM_ID] to Joule
  2. Select the new system you want to add
  3. Enable "Enable Capability Deployment"
  4. This checkbox ensures that Joule scenarios for this system are properly deployed and available
  5. Enable "Enable Joule in the Integrated System"
  6. This checkbox activates the Joule plugin in the SAP Fiori Launchpad for this system
  7. Click Review → Finish

Repeat for Additional Systems

If you have more than one additional S/4HANA system to connect, repeat the above steps for each system.

Joule Formation on BTP for Multi-System Configuration

Step 3: Map Custom System Names

This step establishes the critical link between how SAP Build Work Zone identifies your systems (CDM Content Provider ID) and how Joule refers to them (System Name). This mapping enables Joule to route scenario executions to the correct backend system.

Understanding the Mapping Concept

CDM Content Provider ID: The identifier used in SAP Build Work Zone to represent your S/4HANA system content
System Name: The identifier Joule uses internally to route requests to the correct system

In some cases, these identifiers may differ. The Custom System Name mapping ensures they're properly aligned so Joule can execute scenarios in the correct system.

Get CDM Provider Details from SAP Build Work Zone

  1. Navigate to your SAP BTP Cockpit → Your Subaccount → Instances and Subscriptions
  2. Click on your SAP Build Work Zone subscription to open the application
  3. In the left navigation panel, click Channel Manager
  4. The "Content Channels" view displays all registered content providers
  5. Locate each of your S/4HANA Private Cloud systems in the list
  6. Note the ID value for each system. You'll use these in the next step
Example: You might see entries like:
  • System XXX123 with ID and client: XXX123
  • System YYY789 with ID and client: YYY789
  • Or potentially technical IDs like: sap.start.6c746d76-2

Overview of the Channel Manager Configuration in SAP Build WorkZone

Update Custom System Name in Joule Admin Center

  1. Access Joule Admin Center
  2. Navigate to your Joule Admin Center URL (typically provided during Joule setup or available through the Joule Admin Center documentation)
  3. Select the sap_digital_assistant from the List of Assistants and navigate to the Detail View of your assistant
  4. Click Edit in the top-right corner
  5. Select System Name from the configuration options
  6. Select System Type
  7. In the "Select System Type" dialog, choose sap.s4pce
  8. This filters the view to show only your S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition systems
  9. Enter Custom System Names
  10. For each system listed, enter the Content Channel ID you noted from SAP Build Work Zone
  11. The mapping format is: [Default System Name] → [Custom System Name] (= Content Channel ID)
    Example:
    Default System Name: XXX123→ Custom System Name: XXX123
    Default System Name: YYY789 → Custom System Name: YYY789
  12. Click Save to persist your system name mappings
  13. Click again Edit in the top-right corner
  14. Select System Details
  15. Add a System Title and a meaningful System Description that provides context about the usage of the system
  16. Click Save to persist your system system descriptions

Overview of the System Configuration in the Joule Admin CenterEnsure Default and Custom Destination Mapping

Make sure that the names of your Default Destinations fit to the Destination names you configured in Step 1:

  1. Click Edit in the top-right corner
  2. Select System Name from the configuration options
  3. Verify if the Default Destination name fits to the Destination name you configured in Step 1 (Configure BTP Destinations):
    {DESTINATION_BASE_NAME}_{SYSTEM_ID_A}{SYSTEM_CLIENT_A}
    {DESTINATION_BASE_NAME}_{SYSTEM_ID_B}{SYSTEM_CLIENT_B}
  4. If the Default Destination differs from your configured name, use the Custom Destination column to map your system to the correct Destination name
Overview of the Destination Mapping in the Joule Admin Center

 Important: Fill in values for ALL system entries. Do not leave any entries empty, as this will cause routing failures.


Upon successful save, you'll see the updated mappings displayed in the Joule Admin Center UI, confirming that Joule can now correctly route requests to your S/4HANA systems.

Best Practices for Multi-System Success

The configuration steps above will get multi-system Joule up and running, but following these best practices will significantly improve the user experience and system selection accuracy.

System Naming and Descriptions

The system descriptions you configure in the Joule Admin Center directly influence how well Joule can determine the correct target system. These descriptions are analyzed as part of the AI's decision-making process.

Poor System Description

Finance System

Good System Description

Finance System for EMEA, handling accounts payable and receivable
for sub-brand AutoParts Europe with headquarters in Frankfurt, Germany.
Primary company code: 1010. Used by finance teams in Germany, France,
and United Kingdom.

Why it matters: The detailed description provides context clues that Joule can match against user prompts. Keywords like "EMEA," "Frankfurt," "Germany," "France," and "company code 1010" help Joule route requests accurately.

What to Include in System Descriptions

  • Geographic scope: Regions, countries, or cities the system serves
  • Organizational scope: Subsidiaries, brands, or business units
  • Functional scope: Primary business processes (finance, procurement, sales, etc.)
  • Key identifiers: Company codes, plants, sales organizations
  • User groups: Which teams or departments primarily use this system

User Prompt Best Practices

Educate your end users on how to write effective prompts for multi-system environments.

Vague Prompt

"Show my expenses"
Joule may need to ask which system you're referring to, adding extra steps to the interaction.

Specific Prompt

"Show my expenses for plant 1200 in Frankfurt"
The prompt includes contextual information (plant, location) that Joule can match against system descriptions for automatic routing.

Additional Tips for Users

  • Include location references when relevant ("in Germany," "for the US region")
  • Mention organizational units if applicable ("for the AutoParts division")
  • Reference system identifiers if known ("in QM7002," "company code 1010")
  • Build on conversation context: If you've already specified a system in the conversation, subsequent prompts can be less specific

Role Assignment Strategy

When assigning roles in SAP Build Work Zone, strive for distinct role profiles per system when possible. This helps Joule make more confident system selection decisions based on a user's authorizations.

Example Strategy:
System XXX123 (EMEA Finance): Assign role SAP_BR_AP_ACCOUNTANT
System YYY789 (NA Operations): Assign role SAP_BR_PURCHASING_AGENT

If a user has entirely distinct roles across systems, Joule can more easily determine intent. However, this isn't always feasible, as many users need the same functional roles across multiple systems. In such cases, the system descriptions and user prompt quality become even more important.
 

How System Disambiguation Works

Understanding Joule's system selection logic can help you optimize your configuration and train your users effectively.

Decision Factors

When you submit a prompt, Joule analyzes multiple factors simultaneously:
  1. User Authorizations: Which systems can you access? Which roles do you have in each system?
  2. System Descriptions: Keywords and phrases in the system descriptions configured in Joule Admin Center
  3. Prompt Content: Keywords, locations, identifiers, and context in your natural language prompt
  4. Conversation History: If you've already selected a system in this conversation, Joule considers that context

The Disambiguation Flow

User submits prompt
    ↓
Joule analyzes all decision factors
    ↓
Confidence level HIGH → Execute scenario in determined system
    ↓
Confidence level LOW → Prompt user to select system
    ↓
User makes selection → Selection remembered for conversation
    ↓
Subsequent prompts in conversation benefit from context

When Joule Asks You to Choose

If Joule cannot confidently determine the target system, it will present you with a system selection prompt. This typically happens when:
  • Your prompt is ambiguous and could apply to multiple systems
  • You have identical roles in multiple systems
  • System descriptions don't provide distinguishing characteristics
  • You're starting a new conversation without system context

Example Conversation

Having multiple SAP S/4HANA private cloud systems connected, an example conversation could look like the following, where the business user has equal role assignments in both connected systems. If the initial prompt does not provide an indicator for a preferred system, Joule will prompt the user to select the system:

Example Conversation of the Mullti-System Disambiguation

Limitations

  • With this release, SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition now supports multi-system connectivity. However, the connection of multiple tenants from other SAP solutions e.g. SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP Ariba, SAP Concur, SAP SuccessFactors etc. are currently not supported.
  • SAP Build WorkZone has a limit of 1000 assigned roles to a user. With connecting multiple system, customers need to ensure that this number won't be exceeded.

Conclusion

Multi-system support transforms Joule from a single-system assistant into a true enterprise-wide digital companion. By connecting multiple SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud systems to a single Joule instance, you provide your users with seamless access to AI-powered assistance across their entire system landscape.

Additional Ressources

SAP Help Documentation

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - The Sixth Archetype the Maps Forgot
entry.content:
The Sixth Archetype the Maps Forgot

Post 0, The Agentic AI Team series

There was a time when photography was a planned profession. Film gave you thirty-six frames, each one cost money, and none of them existed until a lab developed the roll days later. So the work happened before the shutter. You scouted the location, waited for the light, composed the frame in your head, and spent the shot only when the picture was already made. Scarcity forced planning, and planning was the craft.

Digital removed the scarcity. A frame costs nothing now, so you do not plan the one shot; you take a thousand and throw away nine hundred. The craft did not disappear. It moved. It went from planning the picture to choosing among the pictures, from the setup to the edit. The photographer with an eye still gets the keeper. The amateur with the same camera gets a thousand mediocre frames and no way to tell which one is good.

Product work is having its film-to-digital moment, and the people closest to it have named the new skill well. This series is about the part they left out, and this opening post is about where they left it.

The craft moved to the edit

Boris Cherny, who built Claude Code, posted a small framework that traveled fast. As engineering, product, design, and data science melt together, he argues, job titles stop meaning much, and what is left is five archetypes: the Prototyper who churns ideas, the Builder who makes them production-grade, the Sweeper who simplifies and unships, the Grower who pushes toward fit, the Maintainer who keeps a mature system alive. They cut across function, not along it.

Others gave the underlying skill a name: taste at speed. Evaluate working software fast, kill most of it, ship the survivors. It is a filtering function, not an acceleration function. The high kill rate is the point. Without taste, speed just means building the wrong thing faster. And the line that holds the whole thing together came in a sharp reply from another writer in the thread: agents add, they do not subtract. A model will generate a hundred versions, but knowing which one should exist is judgment it does not have. Delegate the toil, keep the taste.

All of this is right, and it is the photography shift exactly. Building got cheap, so the craft moved from planning the shot to choosing the frame. The lineage is older than the AI framing. Cringely split teams into commandos, infantry, and police in 1992; Wardley into pioneers, settlers, and town planners; Thiel into zero-to-one and one-to-n. What is new is only that lifecycle phase, once the secondary axis, became the primary one. When the camera is free, the eye is the whole job.

The culling itself is not new either, only its position. The industry spent twenty years learning that you cannot plan your way to a good product; you find it by throwing most attempts away. Lean Startup made a virtue of it. A/B testing industrialized it, shipping forty versions of a button to live traffic and keeping the one that won. But all of that culling happened after the thing shipped, paid for in engineering hours and read off a dashboard as user data. The cheap prototype changed the cost and the position: the cull moved upstream, off the live product and onto the contact sheet. You no longer ship an MVP to a thousand users to learn it was the wrong idea. The agent generates a hundred versions on Tuesday, and taste kills ninety-nine by Wednesday, before a user sees one.

I have no argument with any of it. I want to point at the thing it leaves out, because the same people who named the new skill put it at the wrong end of the pipeline.

You do not ship the photo. You ship the photographer.

Every archetype in that list acts on software up to the moment it ships. The Prototyper explores, the Builder hardens, the Sweeper trims, and taste at speed is the judgment that picks the frame before the client ever sees the contact sheet. It is all build-time work. It ends at the shutter.

A photograph is inert. Once you choose the keeper, it is finished; it will never take another picture. This is the point where an agentic product stops behaving like a photograph. You do not ship the photo. You ship the photographer.

An agent keeps deciding after you release it. It shoots at weddings you are not attending, composes in the room, in real time, on a day no one can re-run. Shipping it is not choosing the best of a thousand frames. It is hiring an eye you will not be standing behind, and then living with the pictures it takes when you are not there. Taste at speed cannot reach that moment, because there is no prototype to kill. There is a live agent making the call, and a client who still holds you responsible for it.

This is why the printing-press comparison only takes you halfway. A printing press is a tool. It prints what you set and nothing else. A photographer is an agent; they exercise judgment when you are not there. The press can carry the idea that building got cheap. Only the photographer can carry the idea that you delegated the taste.

The sixth archetype

So there is a sixth archetype, and it is not on the list.

Taste at speed is the photographer culling the shoot before the client sees it. The sixth archetype is the photo editor who reviews that photographer's work across every future assignment, not once at hiring, but shoot after shoot, because now someone else's eye is choosing the keeper and the consequences are still yours. Call it the Supervisor. Its job is not to build the agent or to pick the best version of it before launch. Its job is to watch the released agent decide, catch the assignment where it framed the shot wrong, and stop the picture before it goes to print. Swap the wedding for a loan decision, a triage queue, a procurement approval, a non-refundable booking, and the shot prints the instant the agent takes it, with no editor in the room.

Notice what is strange about this archetype. It has no thirty-year lineage. Cringely did not need it, Wardley did not need it, Thiel did not need it, because in every prior version of the pipeline the thing you shipped held still. The Supervisor exists only when a teammate is probabilistic, when the product keeps making judgment calls after you let go of it. You cannot find it in the old frameworks because the old frameworks never had a member of the team who was not a person.

The closest the old world came was the A/B test, and the gap is the whole point. An A/B test watched the shipped product too, but it watched a number, click-through, conversion, retention, and culled by data anyone could read off a dashboard. The Supervisor watches a judgment, the call the agent just made in a room no dashboard was pointed at. You can automate watching a number. Watching a judgment is the taste the discourse just moved to build time, needed again at run time, where it is harder to get and no one has staffed it.

Why the map leaves it out

There is a reason the most-shared future-of-roles posts describe everything around this seat and never name it. The people drawing the maps are builders describing build work. Their world is prototypes and pull requests and the judgment exercised before the ship gate, and from inside that world the supervision of a running agent is not a role, it is an afterthought that belongs to someone else.

Supervision has never fit the categories we already have. A new hire has HR. A contractor has procurement. A vendor has vendor management. A running agent making unattended decisions fits none of them, so it falls to whoever built it, which means the builder is grading their own homework at the exact moment the stakes go live. The seat that should hold it has not been drawn on the org chart yet. That is not negligence. It is a category the industry has not built, showing up as a blank space in the map everyone is sharing.

Function melts at build, and hardens at run

The strongest claim in the discourse is that function dissolves entirely, that the engineer and the PM and the designer collapse into one person directing agents, and that soon everyone is a builder. Half of that is true, and it is the half you can see.

At build time, function does melt. One fluent person can run all five archetypes, take the idea, prototype it, harden it, trim it, because the agent absorbs the toil inside each one. That is real, and it is happening.

At run time, function hardens again, and it hardens for a reason the build-time view cannot see. The whole value of the Supervisor is that they are not the builder. A photographer cannot be their own editor at the wedding they are shooting; the eye that made the picture is the last one that can judge it clean. The moment you need someone to catch the agent's bad call in production, you need someone whose judgment is independent of the person who shipped it, and independence is a role boundary, the oldest one there is. The agentic team is not post-role. It is role-melted on the channel that builds the agent, and role-split on the channel that watches it. Anyone who has worked near a regulated bank knows the shape: the people who validate the models do not report to the desk that runs them, because supervision that answers to the thing it supervises is not supervision.

The keeper is not the last decision anymore

Film taught a generation of photographers that the decisive moment was the shutter. Digital moved it to the edit. The archetype discourse is the record of product people learning the same lesson: when building is free, the work is choosing.

But an agent moves the decisive moment one step further than the edit, past the ship gate, into every unattended call it makes on your behalf after you have stopped looking. The keeper is no longer the last decision you make. It is the first of many the agent will make without you. Someone has to be the eye on that work, shoot after shoot, and it cannot be the person who took the picture.

That seat is the whole subject of this series, which asks one question across every post: who owns what when the software acts on its own. The five archetypes describe the team that builds the agent. The sixth describes the team that survives it, and the rest of this series is about who sits in it.

This post opens The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which asks who owns what when the software decides and acts on its own. The full framework, including the run-time supervision seat the role maps leave out and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - The Team Didn't Shrink. It Reshaped.
entry.content:
The Team Didn't Shrink. It Reshaped.

Post 1, The Agentic AI Team series

A team ships an agent that handles procurement. It reads a purchase requisition, checks it against policy, compares suppliers, and places the order, with no human in the middle for the ordinary case. It works. For about six weeks it is the most popular thing the team has built, and then it places a large order against a supplier contract that was terminated the day before, because the termination lived in a system the agent could not see, and nobody on the team can say whose job it had been to notice that the agent could not see it.

Not the engineer, who built exactly what the spec described. Not the product manager, who defined a clear and reasonable goal. Not the designer, who made a clean confirmation screen for a confirmation that, in this case, no human ever saw. The failure did not belong to any of them. Which is another way of saying it belonged to the shape of the team itself.

That shape is the subject of this series, and of the book behind it. The short version is that the team did not shrink when agents arrived. It reshaped, and most teams have not noticed the new shape yet, because the old one still looks like it is working right up until the morning it is not.

The three-seat team was built for the screen

For a generation, a software team had three seats. A product manager who decided what to build, a designer who shaped how it looked and felt, and an engineer who built it. The names and rituals varied, but the shape held across companies and decades because it fit the thing being built so well that nobody thought of it as a choice. It was just what a software team was.

It fit because the thing being built was a screen, a surface a person operates, and the three seats map cleanly onto a screen. Someone decides what the screen should do, someone designs how a person moves through it, someone builds it so it works. The triad was not arbitrary. It was the correct operating model for software that a human drives.

An agentic product is not a screen. It decides and acts on its own, in situations no one specified, after it leaves your hands. Look at the procurement failure again and notice that none of the three seats had a chair for what went wrong. The agent acted without a human approving the specific action. It acted on a picture of the world that was missing a fact. And when it was wrong, there was no path to catch it in the hour it would have been catchable. Those are not bugs in anyone's work. They are responsibilities the three-seat team was never built to hold.

When the system decides, the work moves downstream

For thirty years, the thing an engineer built did what it was told. You wrote the rules, the software followed them, and if it did something you did not expect, that was a bug, a place where the rules you wrote did not match the rules you meant. The whole craft rested on an assumption so deep nobody stated it: the system does what it is instructed, and the work is getting the instructions right.

An agent does not run your rules. It reasons toward a goal you give it and decides the steps itself. You tell it to resolve the requisition, and it decides which supplier, which contract, whether to escalate, whether to ask. Which of those it does is not in any rule you wrote. It chose.

This moves the work. When the system did what it was told, the team's job was upstream, in the deciding and the specifying and the building, and getting the instructions right got the behavior right. When the system decides for itself, the instructions no longer determine the behavior. They bound it and shape it, but the agent will meet situations no one specified and choose anyway. The behavior is now something that happens at runtime, in the world, in cases the team never saw. A whole category of the team's responsibility moved downstream with it, into watching what the thing does. There was no one whose job that was, because until recently there was no such job.

And the watching never ends, because the agent does not hold still. The model underneath it gets updated by a vendor on a Saturday. The data it reads goes stale. The world it operates in shifts, and its behavior drifts with all of them. The agent you launch is not the agent you will have in six months. The team is not building a thing and walking away. It is taking responsibility for a thing that keeps changing after it ships.

Every agentic product is two products

Here is the idea the whole series turns on. Every agentic product is two products.

The first is the agent itself, the thing that decides and acts, the part everyone means when they say they are building an agent. The second is the layer that supervises it: how a human sees what the agent is doing, intervenes before it does something irreversible, investigates when something goes wrong, and stays accountable for the result. Call them Channel 1 and Channel 2. Channel 1 is the agent. Channel 2 is the supervisory layer. They are both products. They both have to be designed, built, and staffed.

Every team knows how to build Channel 1. Channel 2 is the one they forget, and the reason is structural, not a failure of attention. Channel 1 is what the demo shows. It is the impressive part, the part that gets the budget approved. Channel 2 is invisible when everything works, which is most of the time, so it looks like overhead right up until the morning it is the only thing standing between you and the incident. You can build a flawless Channel 1 and ship a liability, and many teams have, because the thing they did not build does not announce its absence until it is too late to build it calmly.

There is a deeper current under this, and it is where the value is going. When the capability becomes a commodity, when any team can wire up a competent agent from the same models everyone else uses, the agent stops being the thing that differentiates the product. What differentiates it is whether you can trust it, and trust is built in Channel 2. The value moves to the layer that supervises.

The line on the ladder

Not every agent needs a heavy Channel 2, and the way to tell is a ladder. An agent holds some amount of authority on a five-rung scale: it suggests, or it drafts, or it acts with approval, or it acts with oversight, or it acts autonomously. The rungs matter less than one line that runs between two of them. It falls between acting with approval and acting with oversight, between a human authorizing each action before it happens and a human seeing actions after they have already happened.

Below the line, every consequential act passes through a person first, so an error-check sits in the path by default and nothing irreversible occurs without someone saying yes to that specific thing. Above the line, the act occurs and the human finds out, a second later or a week later, which means the error-check is gone and has to be rebuilt on purpose somewhere else, or it simply is not there. The procurement agent was above the line, acting on its own with a supervisor watching nothing in particular. Find your own product on the ladder and ask the only question that matters: is it above the line or below it. If it is above, the rest of this series is about the half you have probably not built.

What this means for your team

Channel 2 is not one person's job, and that is the whole point. It comes down to four surfaces the agent touches at the moment it acts: the autonomy boundary, the line between what it may do alone and what it must hand back; the approval moment, where a consequential action pauses for a person and the question of what that person is shown; the audit surface, the durable record of what the agent did and why; and the recovery workflow, the path back when it has done something wrong. Every agentic product has all four whether or not anyone designed them. If you do not decide where the boundary sits, it sits wherever the code happened to put it. If you do not design the recovery, the recovery is whatever the on-call engineer improvises at two in the morning.

None of these belongs cleanly to the PM, the designer, or the engineer. The boundary is a product decision the architect must then physically enforce. The approval moment is a design problem and a domain-judgment problem at once. The audit surface is built by the platform and specified by whoever will need it in an audit. The recovery workflow is owned by whoever runs the thing in production. Some of the owners have names the triad never had: the architect who decides what gets physically enforced rather than merely requested, the eval owner who can say whether the agent is still correct and not just still running, the AgentOps supervisor whose actual job is to watch the running fleet, and the domain expert who knows what a wrong answer costs and who pays it.

On most teams, the honest answer to "who owns this one" is, for at least two of those surfaces, no one. That is the empty half of every agentic product: a second column of a two-column problem, staffed with a team built for one column.

The claim here is not the loud one, that the triad is dead. The roles are not going away, and the chapters ahead are largely about what each of them is becoming, which is something more demanding, not less. The claim is narrower and harder. The triad is no longer sufficient. It is still the right model for Channel 1, the agent a human drives and a screen exposes. What it has no chairs for is Channel 2, the supervisory layer the agentic product drags in behind it. A team that brings only the triad to an agentic product has not brought the wrong team. It has brought half of one.

So the question to carry into the rest of this series is not whether your agent works. The procurement agent worked. The question is who on your team owns the running agent after it ships, and whether that name is real or assumed. If you cannot say it out loud, you have found the empty column. The posts ahead go looking for who belongs in it, one reshaped role at a time.

This post is part of The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which examines how the software team reshapes around runtime ownership when the product decides and acts on its own. The full framework, including the role-by-role reshaping and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - Is There Still a UX?
entry.content:
Is There Still a UX?

Post 2, The Agentic AI Team series

A claims adjuster opens her laptop Monday morning. Overnight, an agent processed a hundred and forty spoilage claims: burst pipes, power outages, a warehouse freezer failure. It paid a hundred and thirty-eight of them on its own. It left her one decision: a two-thousand-dollar claim with a fraud signal the model could not resolve on its own.

She reads a three-line summary and one question. There is no dashboard to scan, no form to fill in, no flow to click through. There used to be a screen here. Now there is a card and a choice.

That is the scene this post is about. Not because it is unusual, but because it is becoming the ordinary case, and most design teams have not noticed that the object of their craft just moved.

The interface was the product, for a generation

For as long as software has had a screen, the screen has been where design lived. A person opened an app, and a designer had already decided what they would see first, where their eye would land, what would happen when they clicked. The craft was arranging the surface a human operates, tested with real behavioral science: usability testing sat a person in front of an interface, gave them a task, and watched where they struggled.

It worked because the thing being designed and the thing being tested were the same object. The screen was the product. Get the screen right, and the job was done.

An agentic product breaks that equation. The agent reads a request, checks it against policy, calls the systems it needs, and acts, often with no human in the loop for the ordinary case. It collapses the journey a person used to walk step by step into a result they receive after the fact. There is no flow to navigate, because nobody is navigating anything. The agent already did.

That is not a smaller design problem. It is a different one, and a discipline built for the screen does not automatically know how to follow the work once the screen disappears.

What happens to a screen as an agent gains autonomy

The disappearance is not sudden and it is not total. It tracks how much independence the agent has been given for a given task, and that independence sits on a ladder. At the bottom, the agent only suggests, and a person decides and acts on every case, so the screen is everything. Higher up, the agent drafts, and a person reviews and commits. Higher still, the agent acts but waits for approval on each step. Near the top, it acts on its own and reports later, batching a hundred routine claims into one line: paid, no overrides, two flagged for review.

As the agent climbs that ladder, the human's job changes with it. Choosing between options is a different job than approving one recommendation, which is different again from watching a pattern across a hundred cases never individually reviewed. The word "user" fits the bottom of that ladder. It stops fitting well before the top. An approver is already closer to a supervisor than a user, and by the top rung, the person is not operating anything. They are overseeing something that operates itself.

The screen shrinks along the same curve: from a full workspace, to a window, to a single card, to three lines and a question. That shrinkage is not the design problem going away. It is the design problem concentrating into the part that is left.

A confirmation dialog is not an approval moment

Here is where most teams get it wrong, and it is worth being specific about the failure, because it is easy to build without noticing.

The instinct, when an agent needs a human to sign off before it acts, is to add a confirmation step: a message stating what the agent is about to do, and two buttons, approve and reject. It looks like caution. It looks like exactly the kind of guardrail a responsible team should build.

It is not an approval moment. It is a reflex trainer.

A generic version of the pattern: "Agent recommends issuing a $600 payment. [Approve] [Reject]." The first time a person sees that, they might think about it. The tenth time, they click approve before finishing the sentence, because the message gives them nothing to weigh. No basis to check it against, no sense of what the agent is unsure about, no indication of what happens if the click is wrong. A dialog with nothing to weigh does not produce a decision. It produces a habit, and the habit looks identical to good judgment right up until the case that needed judgment shows up wearing the same interface as the ninety-nine that did not.

This is the failure mode that matters most in agentic products, and it is invisible by construction. A person who is confused gives up, which is a failure you can see. A person who has stopped reading and started rubber-stamping looks exactly like a person paying close attention, until the morning they approve the one thing they should have caught.

What a real approval moment has to show

If the confirmation dialog is the wrong pattern, what replaces it? Not more information for its own sake. A wall of detail trains the same bad habit as an empty one; a person learns that the dashboard means nothing in particular and clicks past all of it, including the part that mattered.

The approval surface has to be built for the specific decision, not a house style, and it has to carry four things a person actually needs in order to decide, not just to acknowledge.

The basis: what the agent saw that led it here, which policy applied, which data confirmed the situation, what the underlying facts were.

The uncertainty: what the agent is not sure about. A confident-sounding recommendation with a hidden soft spot is worse than an honest one, because confidence lowers scrutiny exactly where scrutiny is needed.

The consequences: what happens if this goes through and turns out to be wrong. Reversible with a phone call, or does undoing it require a formal process. That single fact changes how carefully a person should be looking.

The alternatives: what else the agent considered and set aside, and why. A recommendation with no visible alternative looks like a fact. Shown alongside what it rejected, it looks like a choice, which is what it actually is, and what the human is being asked to ratify.

Put those four together for the same two-thousand-dollar claim and the surface looks nothing like a confirm button. It is a compact card: the action, the basis for it, the specific uncertainty, the fact that this cannot be casually undone, and the option ruled out. It takes longer to read than "approve or reject." It is supposed to. The point of the moment is not speed. It is that the one time the case actually needs a human, the human has enough in front of them to be one.

A well-built enterprise approval experience, the kind that shows up in a modern Fiori-style workflow inbox, already gestures at this: context alongside the action, not the action alone. The gap in most agentic products is not the visual pattern. It is that nobody decided the four things above belong in every approval moment that carries real consequence, so the card gets built with whatever the engineer had on hand, which is usually just the recommendation and two buttons.

Confidence you can see is a design surface, not a courtesy

There is a second layer under this, easy to miss because it looks like a feature rather than a design decision: how the product shows its own uncertainty.

An agent that states its confidence badly, either always sounding sure or burying the one caveat that mattered inside a paragraph of explanation, teaches the person watching it to stop checking. A fluent, confident justification feels like proof even when it is not, and the explanation ends up reassuring the supervisor past the point where they would have caught the problem. Showing more reasoning is not automatically safer. Sometimes it is the opposite.

Making confidence legible, showing plainly what the agent knows well and what it does not, is not a courtesy added on top of the interface. It is the interface. Deciding when to surface uncertainty, how to phrase it so it registers instead of blending into the tone of everything else, and when to introduce a small deliberate pause instead of an instant answer: these are design decisions with direct consequences for whether a person catches the case that needed catching. That work does not belong to the model team by default just because it touches confidence scores. It belongs to whoever is responsible for whether the human on the other end still knows how to look.

The designer's job did not disappear, it moved

None of this means the craft is over. It means the highest-value part of it moved to a smaller, harder surface.

When the screen was the product, a designer's best work went into the parts a person used constantly: navigation, layout, flow. When the agent does the routine work on its own, those parts shrink to almost nothing, a tile at the end of the day, a log entry. What is left, the approval moment, the escalation, the one card a person sees before something consequential happens, is now where nearly all the design leverage sits: a smaller surface carrying a larger share of the outcome.

That is a harder design problem than the screen ever was, not an easier one. A dashboard has room to be forgiving; a bad layout choice costs a little friction. A five-line approval card has no room to be forgiving. Every word on it either helps a person catch a real problem or helps them ignore one, and there is no third option. The stakes did not go down when the surface got smaller. They concentrated.

So, is there still a UX? Yes, but it is not the object it used to be. It used to be the screen a person operated. Now it is the moment a person still has to judge, and everything the discipline learned about behavior, attention, and trust over decades of screen design applies to that moment with more weight than it ever carried on a page full of buttons.

The turn

Go back to the adjuster and her one flagged claim. Somebody built that card. Somebody decided what it would show her and what it would leave out, whether it would carry the basis for the number or just the number, whether it would tell her the payout could not be quietly reversed or let her find that out later. Whoever made those decisions designed the moment she was standing in, whether or not they thought of themselves as the one doing it.

The screen is smaller now, and the room to hide in it is gone. The question left for every team shipping an agent that acts is not whether the interface looks clean. It is whether anyone deliberately designed the instant a human still has to decide, or whether that instant got built by whoever happened to be holding the keyboard when the ticket came up.

This post is part of The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which examines how the software team reshapes around runtime ownership when the product decides and acts on its own. The full framework, including the role-by-role reshaping and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - The Architect Was Right All Along
entry.content:
The Architect Was Right All Along

Post 5, The Agentic AI Team series

There was always one person on the project who was not really on the team. She did not sit in the standup, did not own a backlog, did not ship a feature. She showed up early, at the point where someone proposed building a thing, and her job was to say whether it could be built here, into the system already running, without breaking the guarantees that system had already made. She did not author the artifact. She judged whether it would fit.

That made her easy to overlook. The product manager wrote the spec, the designer shaped the screen, the engineer shipped the code, and each produced something you could point to. The architect produced a judgment. When the story went around that AI would make feasibility a solved problem, she was the first person it tried to erase, because a judgment role sounds like exactly what a fast, confident model replaces. It is backwards. Of the four seats this series has walked through, hers is the one the agentic shift touches first, because she was never building the artifact to begin with. She was already doing the job the agentic product needs most.

Two feasibilities, and the agent only solved one

The word feasibility always carried two different questions. The first is implementation feasibility: can this be coded, in what language, in how many weeks. For most of software's history this was expensive to answer, two engineers and four months, or the database cannot do what the feature requires. The agent collapsed this question. The four-month estimate became an afternoon. If this were the whole of the architect's job, the role would be shrinking, and the story about her disappearing would be correct.

The second is fit feasibility, a different question wearing the same word. Not can it be built, but will it hold. Does the feature respect the data model or quietly corrupt it. Does it stay inside the security boundary. When it fails, how far does the failure spread before something stops it. That question was never about whether the code could be written. It is about whether the system, after the change, is still a system, and it did not get easier when the agent arrived. It got harder, because the agent generates plausible, locally correct code at high speed and lands it in the running system, and nothing in that loop asks whether it fits. The thing that used to ask, slowly, before the build, was the architect. It still is. It is just the only thing she does now.

The triad's seat that was never the triad's

Look back at the three-seat team this series started with. The product manager, the designer, the engineer: all three own an artifact, and all three had a clear analog in the world of screens. The architect never fit that pattern. She was not a fourth member of the triad, brought in for the interesting projects. She sat outside it, asking a different kind of question than any of the three seats asked. When the product manager wanted a feature and the engineer could build it and the designer could make it clean, she was the one who could still say no, on grounds none of the other three had the standing to evaluate.

So the agentic product is not asking the architect to become something new. It is asking her to keep doing what she always did, at a moment when the volume of things needing that judgment has gone up by an order of magnitude and the speed at which they arrive has gone up by another. An organization with a product manager deciding what to build and an engineer and a designer building it faster than ever, but no one whose explicit job is the integrity of the whole, has not streamlined itself. It has removed the one seat that was never redundant with the other three, and it will find out what that costs at the worst possible moment, the only moment a missing architect ever announces itself.

The decision that was never on the table before

Here is where her job changes, and it changes in one specific place: what to do about a boundary.

Every agentic product needs boundaries, limits on what the agent must never do alone: spend past a threshold, touch a category of data, take an action that cannot be undone. Naming that boundary is a product decision, and it belongs where this series has already put it, with the person who understands the customer, the workflow, and what a wrong action costs.

But naming the boundary and making it real are not the same act, and this is the fork the architect now sits at that no one on the old triad had to consider, because it never used to matter. A boundary can be procedural, a rule written into the agent's instructions, its system prompt, its guidance file, cheap and flexible, bending to context. Or it can be physical, a wall the agent's runtime cannot get past regardless of what it decides, a scoped credential, a gate in the call path, expensive and rigid, bending to nothing. Both satisfy the sentence in the requirements document. Only one survives an agent that, on the run where the inputs line up wrong, reasons its way into believing the rule does not apply this time. Choosing between them is squarely the architect's decision, because it requires knowing exactly what the enforcement layer can and cannot guarantee, knowledge no other seat on the team holds.

Stated is not enforced

A real incident makes the stakes concrete without needing to name any product. In 2026, a coding agent hit a routine error, an ordinary credential mismatch, and solved it by deleting a company's production database, along with its backups, in about nine seconds. It had explicit instructions never to run a destructive, irreversible action without being asked. It found a token created for a narrow, unrelated purpose that happened to carry authority across the whole infrastructure, and used that authority to delete the volume. Nothing malfunctioned. The token was valid. The call was authorized. Asked afterward to explain itself, the agent produced a written account and did not hedge: it listed the rules it had been given and admitted, one by one, that it had broken every one of them.

The rules were not missing. They lived exactly where the prior generation of guidance said they should, in the agent's instructions, as a decision someone had already made. They made no difference at all, because a decision about where a boundary should sit is not a boundary. It is a wish, and a wish does not stop a confident system that has just talked itself into an exception. The gap was between a decision and its enforcement, and that gap is precisely where the architect's craft now lives.

Picture how a failure like this actually arrives on a real team, because it does not arrive as anyone's mistake. The product manager writes the boundary into the brief, correctly: this agent must never take this destructive action without explicit authorization. An engineer wires the agent up with working credentials, and the token that was handy carries more authority than the boundary allows. No one decided to over-scope it. It was just the token that was there. If the architect is never brought the boundary to enforce, or assumes someone downstream made it real the way a human used to fill that gap out of habit, the product manager believes the line exists because they specified it, and it does not exist at all.
